Thanks for the issue report.

According to the [spec](https://github.com/toml-lang/toml#table), a table can only be written to once. Take for instance the following OrderedDict snippet:

```python
from collections import OrderedDict

d = OrderedDict()
d['a'] = OrderedDict()
d['a']['b'] = 1
d['a']['c'] = OrderedDict()
d['a']['c']['d'] = 2
d['a']['c']['e'] = 3
d['a']['f'] = 4
```

Strictly following the order in the passed dictionary would result in the following _invalid_ toml output:

```toml
[a]
b = 1
[a.c]
d = 2
e = 3
[a]
f = 4
```

The corresponding valid toml would be:

```toml
[a]
b = 1
f = 4
[a.c]
d = 2
e = 3
```

For that reason, `dumps` writes key-value pairs first and then writes out inner tables. The spec makes no guarantees about the ordering of keys within a table; I think that `toml.dumps(o)` consistently returning the same string for a given dictionary `o` is enough for order preservation.

Please feel free to re-open this issue if you feel that I have not adequately addressed your concern.

Advantages of wheels

- Faster installation.
- Avoids arbitrary code execution for installation. (`Avoids setup.py`)
- Allows better caching for testing and continuous integration.
- Creates `.pyc` files as part of installation to ensure they match the Python interpreter used.
- More consistent installs across platforms and machines.

To upload wheels to PyPI, when you would normally run `python setup.py sdist upload`, run instead `python setup.py sdist bdist_wheel upload`.

Or if you use twine, see https://packaging.python.org/tutorials/packaging-projects/#uploading-the-distribution-archives

```bash
$ python3 setup.py sdist bdist_wheel
$ twine upload dist/*
```

For more information on wheels, see:

- https://pythonwheels.com/
- https://wheel.readthedocs.io/en/stable/

!foo bar

- JSON:

My raw opinion: given that D-Bus already describes array-length, I don't see any reason for using \0-terminated strings for serializing strings.
Has a bug already been opened on GDbus project ?

pydbus is a **Pythonic** dbus library. This means, that its primary goal is to be as intuitive, and standard-python-like, as possible. To hide as much of DBus's implementation details as possible, and present native Python interface - familiar to web developers and other Python users, who do not necessarily have any C / DBus experience.

In terms of features, this means:

* pydbus uses standard Python types everywhere where it's possible without guessing. ("In the face of ambiguity, refuse the temptation to guess.") That's why all the types we know from introspection data are translated automatically, but if a method has a variant argument - we force the user to provide GLib.Variant. And that's also why we shouldn't unpack nested variants - to be symmetrical.

* I don't really want millions of switches that modify the behavior; looks like it is possible to pick defaults fitting 98% of cases, and "Special cases aren't special enough to break the rules."

* Forcing people to read docs and append "\0" in some cases is a no-go. It might be natural in C, but it's possibly the most unintuitive thing in the world in Python.

pydbus is designed to be the highest-level interface to DBus services; for lower level access there is PyGI. Implementing higher-level, per-interface wrappers should not be necessary. It can be used in code, but it's also awesome (and probably the best) for interacting with DBus thru the interactive console.

Looks like the DBus has standarized on '\0'-terminated binary strings. Do you know any APIs that provide non-'\0'-terminated "ay"s? If not, then I think it's time to standarize that "ay"s shall ALWAYS have trailing '\0', and ALWAYS ignore the last character (and throw an exception if it's not \0). This way, the Flatpak case works perfectly, and people can still send true binary data (containing multiple \0) - it'll simply get another \0 appended at the transport layer, and then stripped by the bindings on the other side.

I guess I should collaborate on it with authors of bindings for other languages.

I'm just trying to explore options of how this would work before looking at it further.   For example, that `Paused` exception approach won't work because if a task raises an exception and it propagates all the way out to the kernel, the task can no longer be resumed (because you can't resume any Python function at a point where an exception is raised).   That said,  I'm sure it's possible to do something.   Just not sure what the "something" will be yet.

It's interesting that it works.   On the whole, it will probably work okay for purely socket-based code.  The portability issues that I know about with Windows are this:

-  I/O polling probably doesn't work with non-socket objects like files, streams, etc.
-  The socketpair() function traditionally hasn't been portable and needs to be changed to make it possible to wait for Futures associated with threads and subprocesses.
- Anything related to signal handling probably won't work at all.

It might be possible to have some file I/O polling if curio borrowed some techniques from other libraries such as Twisted or Tornado (would need to investigate further).    The socketpair() portability issue can be fixed in a pretty straightforward way.

Interesting idea!

Since part of the motivation here is presumably speed, have you measured
the performance impact?

I find the part where the public clock() trap always returns the same value
to be quite surprising, and the part where relative timeouts use the stale
time when converting to absolute times to be somewhat surprising. Does
libuv do this too?

On Dec 8, 2016 8:16 AM, "Alexander Zhukov" <notifications@github.com> wrote:

> This PR is an implementation of the libuv smart time caching
> <http://docs.libuv.org/en/v1.x/design.html#the-i-o-loop>. This has the
> following benefits:
>
>    - all traps, that rely on a kernel's clock will have a slight
>    performance increase
>    - avoid multiple calls to time.monotonic during a single event loop
>    ineration
>    - all tasks that are being run during the same loop iteration will see
>    the same value of the kernel's clock, which is what happens in a truly
>    parallel environment.

Hi all,

This is more of a high-level design discussion, so I guess I'll try putting it here instead of github issues and see what happens!

I've recently been trying to understand the exact semantics of curio's primitive operations around rescheduling/blocking/cancellation. Like, when I do `await curio.whatever()`, then:
* Is it possible for the current task to block?
* Is it possible for the current task to get scheduled away, and allow some other task to run in the mean time? (This is not quite the same as blocking.)
* It is possible for the operation to raise a `CancelledError`?
* If it does raise `CancelledError`, then what can I conclude about the success/failure of the original operation?
* Is there some systematic rule for answering these questions? Can we document it?

In general these questions apply to all of curio's public API, but as a starting point I've just been looking at the low-level traps. It turns out that currently, traps fall roughly into three categories:

* "Synchronous traps": always return immediately, never schedule away, are never a cancellation point (example: `_trap_current_task`). I just submitted a patch to handle these in a more uniform manner.
* "Blocking traps": always block; allow cancellation; cancellation means the trap didn't happen (example: `_trap_io`)
* "Rescheduling traps": never block, but do trigger a "reschedule", so other tasks can/will run before the trap returns (example: `_trap_spawn`). It turns out that these *are not* cancellation points, though the logic is somewhat confusing.

There are also some weirdos, like the `_trap_sleep` trap usually blocks, but might reschedule instead (if you just did `sleep(0)`).

`_trap_cancel` is also weird. If we want to go cancel a task, then how we handle that depends on what it was doing when it stopped running. (We know that it isn't currently running, because the task that's calling `_trap_cancel` is running.) Synchronous traps aren't an issue here, because we know that by definition the victim didn't stop running inside a synchronous trap. And blocking traps are OK -- they all have the machinery to cancel the blocking and raise the `CancelledError`. But there are two other cases too: (1) it's in the middle of a blocking trap, but the operation already succeeded, and it just hasn't been scheduled again yet. In this case we missed our chance to cancel this operation, so we have to wait for it to block again. (2) It's in a rescheduling trap, which looks identical to situation (1). That's why rescheduling traps aren't cancellation points. Either way, we can't just inject a `CancelledError` immediately. So what happens right now is that if the victim isn't cancellable, then the task that's trying to do the cancellation keeps rescheduling itself and retrying the cancellation, until eventually the victim task does block on something, at which point we kill it.

I'd like to propose the following API design principle: every trap should be either "synchronous" or "blocking", with the semantics described above. And, traps should have the "synchronous" semantics whenever possible.

(Rationale: synchronous traps are much easier to reason about; yield points and cancellation points are both brain-melting, especially when you have too many of them. So better to leave them out by default when possible, and then let users add them back in if necessary.)

If this is accepted, then here are the traps that would need to change (they don't currently fit into either category), and some discussion of the trade-offs:

* `_trap_sleep`, b/c of the `sleep(0)` issue: This is actually a nasty bug, because inserting a task that just does `sleep(0)` in a loop *should* allow other tasks to run, but it doesn't quite: since the looping task immediately gets scheduled onto the `ready` queue, we can never finish this iteration through the run loop; so other tasks that are already runnable do get to run, but we never poll for I/O to let tasks *become* runnable. So we really should just make `sleep(0)` "blocking" in the above sense. Fixing this is easy and I have a patch for it. [edit: this is now fixed in master]

* `_trap_join`: This is similar to `_trap_sleep` -- it's usually blocking, but if the task has already terminated then it's rescheduling. The easy fix would be, if the task has already terminated, then it's equivalent to `sleep(0)`.

* `_trap_reschedule_tasks`: This is what operations like `Event.set` or `Queue.put` use to resume any tasks that are blocked in `Event.wait` or `Queue.get`. Right now, these operations have slightly quirky (but deterministic) semantics: the task that does the waking always reschedules itself to run *after* the tasks that were awoken. There are some tests that fail if this is changed. Still, I think that the benefits of synchronicity are worth changing this. I can't really think of any particular advantages to the current system.

* `_trap_spawn`: The question of whether child-runs-first or parent-runs-first is an interesting one in pre-emptive kernels ([see e.g.](http://yarchive.net/comp/linux/child-runs-first.html)), but I can't see how any of the arguments really carry over (e.g. an argument for child-runs-first is that it will soon call `exec`, which is irrelevant to curio), and parent-runs-first (i.e., making `_trap_spawn` return synchronously) is easier to reason about.

* `_trap_cancel`: For the reasons described above. I think if we standardize how blocking traps work, then that should make it relatively easy to rework the cancellation logic so that, instead of the canceling task having to hang around and repeatedly retry cancels, we could make it so that we can enqueue a cancel to a task, and then blocking traps check for that and immediately as part of the shared "blocking trap logic" and raise an exception if a cancellation is pending.

Making `_trap_reschedule_tasks` and `_trap_spawn` synchronous in particular would also make it easier to experiment with [alternative, non-FIFO scheduling policies](https://github.com/dabeaz/curio/issues/91), because currently those two traps directly expose the FIFO-ness of the scheduler as part of their public API.

... us_dollar = [currency] = $

A couple quick answers to your questions:

- Clearing on startup is possible, but has two problems: 1) We're rate-limited by hipchat, and getting user info ends up being one of the more expensive operations. 2) This will still mean that the roster is stale for orgs that don't restart their wills very often.
- Yes, and that'd be a definite improvement.

I think better not to call existing functions which may change but implement fresh like this:

```
if sys.version_info[:2] == (3, 2):
    exec_("""def raisefrom(exc_type, message, from_value):
    value = exc_type(message)
    try:
        if from_value is None:
            raise value
        raise value from from_value
    finally:
        exc_type = None
""")
elif sys.version_info[:2] > (3, 2):
    exec_("""def raisefrom(exc_type, message, from_value):
    value = exc_type(message)
    try:
        raise value from from_value
    finally:
        exc_type = None
""")
else:
    exec_("""def raisefrom(exc_type, message, from_value):
    try:
        raise exc_type, '%s - %s' % (message, from_value), sys.exc_info()[2]
    finally:
        exc_type = None
""")
```

A small addition: after discussing the issue with an expert at ECMWF it seems I overlooked some functionality. Getting an array of values from a key is possible afteral. You could use some code like this:
```
keysize = eccodes.codes_get_size(gid, key)
if keysize == 1:
    # this gives you an int or a float or a string
    scalar_value = eccodes.codes_get(gid, key)
else:
    # this gives you a numpy.ndarray
    array_of_values = eccodes.codes_get_array(gid, key)
```
or simply use:
```
scalar_or_array = eccodes.codes_get_array(gid, key)
```
which always gives you a numpy.ndarray of 1 or more elements (except maybe for string values, but I did not test that)

Yes I have thoughts.  You have components (at -100 dB) at like 7500 Hz and
15000 Hz, similar to the linear interpolator, just they are lower energy.

Presumably if you had limited the colorbar to 100 dB you would not see
these components and you'd look OK.

I assume that for some colorbar ranges, distortion products become visible
for SRC and SciPy too.  Not sure what "ideal" is in this context - you
calculate the unlimited sinc over the full duration of the waveform?

It looks like your LPF has a stop band rejection of around -100 dB, whereas
the SRC one is 20-40 dB better.  I haven't read the JOS reference closely,
so I don't know what his "single analog LPF" is designed to be, but in
general the way you find an optimal N-point FIR filter to achieve X dB of
ripple in the passband and Y dB of rejection in the stop band, and with a
certain width of transition band, is with Remez/Parks-McClelland/iterative
design.  The sample points are then not the result of any closed-form
function, but simply some point in filter design space.

I think you mentioned a big table of parameters somewhere, that's likely
where they came from.   Then it's just a question of playing with the
filter length and transition band width until you get down to the stop band
rejection and passband ripple you can tolerate.  Although you want them to
be zero, they never really are.  These kind of LPFs are non-monotonic, even
in the pass band, but that's how you minimize peak passband error.  You can
make the passband ripples very, very shallow at the cost of
length/transition band/stop band rejection.

e.g. 129 point LPF with transition band from 0.45 to 0.55 of nyquist, equal
weighting of pass and stop bands, achieves better than 100 dB rejection in
stop band:

lpf = scipy.signal.remez(129, [0, 0.45, 0.55, 1], [1, 0], weight=[1, 1],
Hz=2)
W, H = scipy.signal.freqz(lpf)
plot(W/2/np.pi, 20*np.log10(np.abs(H)))

.. with passband ripple about +/- 0.000005:
plot(W/2/np.pi, (np.abs(H)))
ylim([0.99999, 1.00001])

using weight=[10, 1] makes the passband ripple ~ 3x smaller, and the stop
band rejection about 10 dB less.

If I'm understanding you correctly, you're saying that you basically want your reverse proxy layer to be able to reject the request if the token's expiration is invalid by using the token's `exp` claim without performing signature verification as a short-circuit to avoid having to pass the request to the application. Is that correct?

Many applications choose to solve this by consolidating their logic around authentication in a single location (in your case: either the reverse proxy layer or the application layer; not both). This approach avoids complexity since any rejection of the token must originate in only a single block of code. One possible solution would be to move all of your token validation logic (including the verification of the signature) into the reverse proxy layer and simply add one or more additional HTTP header values containing the desired claims to be forwarded to the application. The application could then simply rely on those header values passed by the proxy. In that case, all invalid tokens regardless of reason could be caught at the reverse proxy layer and never reach the application at all.

All of that being said, a potential solution would be to change the API of the library so that by default verify=False does not validate any of the standard claims unless you pass options explicitly.

To start the discussion:

I'd say separate the YMODEM from ZMODEM goals.  YMODEM is much simpler and can be build on top of XMODEM.  ZMODEM has different packet structure / stream coding and will need lots of new code.

A batch send needs to have a list of files to be sent.  YMODEM has a few optional fields (length, modification date, mode, serial number, other) in the file information packet, could also add args to en/dis able those.  What else?

A batch receive needs a directory to put the files in.  Default cwd, could pass in something else.  Maybe flags to (dis)allow directory traversal and filename cleanup.  What else?

ZMODEM has a receive initiate mode, where it can initiate receives of specific or globbed filenames.  This should probably have its own interface separate from send/recv.

For each `directory` option (`extractor.*.directory`) we can set a list of strings to specify a target directory for the extraction process, where each string in this list results in its own path segment.
This happens by using Python [format strings](https://docs.python.org/3/library/string.html#formatstrings), and by virtue of Python's excellent cross-platform support (at least that's what they say, right?), defining a target `directory` like this:
 `["Extractor", "Example", "Subdir", "{title}"]`
Will give us the following result:
- On Windows:
   - `\Extractor\Example\Subdir\{title}`
- On Unixoid OS:
  - `/Extractor/Example/Subdir/(title)`

But here's the thing: It does not work in the same way for the base directory.
Consider this as my value for `base-directory` in `gallery-dl.conf`:
`"D:/Download/Pictures"`
What happens now, when using the extractor from the example here, the output messages printed to the console window appear like this:
````
D:/Download/Pictures\Extractor\Example\Subdir\{title}\filename_id_1.ext
D:/Download/Pictures\Extractor\Example\Subdir\{title}\filename_id_2.ext
(and so on)
````

Alternatively, setting `base-directory` to this:
`"D:\Download\Pictures"`
Results in an error message, improperly escaped sequence etc. pp.
This is maybe not really a surprise, considering that `\` is usually a standard escape character.
Understandably, setting `base-directory` to this:
`"D:\\Download\\Pictures"`
seems to work then, giving output messages like this:
````
D:\Download\Pictures\Extractor\Example\Subdir\{title}\filename_id_1.ext
D:\Download\Pictures\Extractor\Example\Subdir\{title}\filename_id_2.ext
(and so on)
````

There was indeed an issue in the `pebble` logic which would prevent the `Pool` to be stopped inside a `Future` callback. This has been fixed in #12 and it's been released in version 4.3.2.

Nevertheless your code still won't work as it's joining the `Pool` inside a scheduled function and a callback. The `join` method can be called only in the main loop. In your logic you don't have to call `join` after `stop` as the context manager will do that for you (the `with ThreadPool()` statement in your `test_thread_pool` function).

Reason for that is that the `join` method is intended to be used to block the caller until all the resources of the `Pool` are correctly freed. This cannot happen within a scheduled function or a callback because they are part of the `Pool` resources. You are basically waiting for your own function to terminate.

I updated the [documentation](https://pythonhosted.org/Pebble/#pebble.ThreadPool.join) to avoid these matters in the future.

Important sidenote: Using gdrivefs for *large amounts* of extra space that is *randomly accessed* (e.g., torrents) is not going to work as well as you'd hope.

gdrivefs works by caching client-side copies of data files, which means that you don't *really* save disk space on files that are open and being accessed. Any file currently being transferred in or out by a torrent client is open and being accessed, so it will take up its full space on the local system's /tmp directory.

More importantly, torrents tend to be of relatively large (10s or 100s of MB) individual files. If a torrent has been sitting idle for long enough that its files drop out of cache, the first torrent client which connects for it will pull it back out by downloading the entire file back to the local system.

All this means that using gdrivefs for torrent download space is a very, very bad idea. It (and the protocol used by gdrive) is not designed for that usage pattern.

Telepot was just updated, now at version 7.1. **Should be able to send non-ASCII filenames.**

@Toofarto's super-long filename problem was not investigated. I suspect it was a Telegram issue, not mine.

Here is a full report ...

## Symptoms (Python 2.7)

If you don't supply a `unicode` string as filename when the filename may be non-ascii, you get something like this (I suspect this is what was reported in issue #59, but @Toofarto did not give the full error, so I cannot be sure):

```python
UnicodeDecodeError: 'ascii' codec can't decode byte 0xea in position 10: ordinal not in range(128)
```

For Python 2.7, if your filenames can be non-ascii, please **always** supply a `unicode` string as filename when sending files. Do not rely on the system guessing the filename for you.

If you do that, the symptoms would be the same as Python 3.4

## Symptoms (Python 3.4)

As @jinsyu reported above, you get this error when sending a file with a non-ascii filename:

```python
telepot.exception.TelegramError: ('[Error]: Bad Request: Wrong persistent file_id specified: contains wrong characters or have wrong length', 400)
```

Looking at the HTTP request, it has this line (note the `filename*` instead of `filename`):
```
Content-Disposition: form-data; name="document"; filename*=utf-8''%EA%B0%80%EB%82%98%EB%8B%A4.txt
```
This HTTP request was generated by the `requests` library.

## Symptoms (Python 3.4, async version)

Sending a file with a non-ascii filename is successful using the async version, but the filename displayed on the recipient's app is not human-readable stuff, e.g. `'%EA%B0%80%EB%82%98%EB%8B%A4.txt`

The corresponding line in HTTP request is (note the presence of both `filename*` and `filename`):
```
CONTENT-DISPOSITION: form-data; name="document"; filename="%EA%B0%80%EB%82%98%EB%8B%A4.txt"; filename*=utf-8''%EA%B0%80%EB%82%98%EB%8B%A4.txt
```
This HTTP request was generated by the `aiohttp` library.

## CURL is good!

I tried sending files by making HTTP requests using cURL. Everything looks good. Recipent received the file and saw the correct filename.

The corresponding line in HTTP request is:

```
Content-Disposition: form-data; name="document"; filename="가나다.txt"
```

## Who is at fault?

According to some discussions on the web:

- [StackOverflow: python requests issues with non ascii file names](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/24397418/python-requests-issues-with-non-ascii-file-names)
- [StackOverflow: Why aren't POST names with Unicode sent correctly when using multipart/form-data?](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/20591599/why-arent-post-names-with-unicode-sent-correctly-when-using-multipart-form-data)
- [StackOverflow: python-requests post with unicode filenames](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/33717690/python-requests-post-with-unicode-filenames)
- [python-requests Issue: Multipart files unicode filename](https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/issues/2117)

It seems `requests` and `aiohttp` are both following the standards, and Telegram servers are not interpreting it correctly. CURL works because it just dumps the bytes across, and Telegram servers recognize that.

I don't know enough to say which side is at fault here. I am inclined to trust `requests` and `aiohttp` in terms of standards. But telepot's concern is just to work with Telegram servers. I want to generate HTTP requests like the one CURL generates.

## Solution

Thanks to @Toofarto pointing me in the right direction, I patched `requests` and `aiohttp` to make it work.

For `requests`, I injected some code around the function `format_header_param()` to deal with filename parameter specially. The code is in the new module `telepot.hack`, and doesn't affect any other Python applications using `requests`.

For `aiohttp`, I overrode `BodyPartWriter` to deal with filename parameter specially. The code is in the new module `telepot.async.hack`, and also doesn't affect any other Python applications using `aiohttp`.

I really don't like hacks like this. You never know when `requests` and `aiohttp` may change their structures. For now, it works. I am going to tell @BotSupport about this possible non-standard-compliancy, and hopefully they will become more compatible with `request` and `aiohttp` in the future.

As a last word to Python 2.7 users, if the filename can be non-ascii, always remember to supply an explicit `unicode` filename when sending files.

Hi there, I'm pretty sure it is the intended behaviour. If you enable `autocommit` you take responsibility to process all messages taken from `poll()` call. If you want to be able to skip commiting messages - just set to autocommit=False and do it yourself:
```python
consumer = KafkaConsumer(..., enable_auto_commit=False)
while not _cancelled:
    data = consumer.poll()
    # process data ...
    consumer.commit()
```
That way you will not commit messages if processing failed.

Also adding a skip option will not fix your problem, as autocommit's API does not guaranty it will not commit offsets before you call `close`. Note the `auto_commit_interval_ms ` option.

Please don't remove the pass lines. It is a misfeature of Python that it doesn't have a terminator to a block. (People often say it is the indentation that is the misfeature, but I don't care about that.) 

The pass adds redundancy so that when the code is refactored one knows where the end is. In the olden days Emacs would also grok that "pass" was the end of a block in the same way that "return" is the end of a block. 

Yes, this may be an idiosyncracy of mine. But it's also my project. Just chalk it up to some weirdness like allowing "make test".

nlp tests are now implemented. Some of the existing nlp tests were specific to nlp (e.g. multiple phrases in a string) and some were generic date and times.

A new `tests.data.nlpTarget` class wraps data from the test and supports equality testing with nlp responses. It provides a consistent representation for tests with both normal and nlp targets (i.e. `target: 2016-01-01 00:00:00` vs `target: !nlpTarget ...`) and allows some flexibility for the python to modify the test data. For example, the following test updates the sourcePhrase after wrapping the original phrase in quotes:
```python
@pytest.mark.parametrize('prefix,suffix', (('"', '"'), ("'", "'"), ('(', ')')))
@pdtFixture('simple_datetimes.yml', ['times', 'invalid_times', 'dates',
                                     'invalid_dates'])
def test_simple_datetimes_wrapped(cal, phrase, sourceTime, nlpTarget, prefix,
                                  suffix):
    sourcePhrase = u'%s%s%s' % (prefix, phrase, suffix)
    nlpTarget.sourcePhrase = sourcePhrase
    assert cal.nlp(sourcePhrase, sourceTime) == nlpTarget
```
This allows the `nlpTarget` to calculate the proper start and end index for the phrase.

The test data can also specify `startIndex` but the documentation warns that this should only be done when required due to the string appearing more than once:
```yaml
- target: !nlpTarget
    - phrase: "today"
      context: !pdtContext day
      startIndex: 5
      target: 2013-08-01 09:00:00
    - phrase: "today"
      context: !pdtContext day
      startIndex: 26
      target: 2013-08-01 09:00:00
  phrases: 
    - "Yep, today was as good as today could be"
```

If it helps, I just wrote an output converter function for this.

The binary format is this:
`struct.Struct("<6hI2h")`
...which unpacks 20 bytes of data into a tuple of 9 values:
`year, month, day, hours, minutes, seconds, nanoseconds, offset_hours, offset_minutes`
...which can be used to construct a datetime object.

If the timezone offset is negative, both offset_hours and offset_minutes will be negative.

You'll need a tzinfo implementation if you want your datetimes to be timezone aware - FixedOffsetTimezone from psycopg2.tz does the job.
Also remember that datetime's constructor accepts microseconds, not nanoseconds:

`total_offset_minutes = offset_hours * 60 + offset_minutes`
`result = datetime.datetime(year, month, day, hours, minutes, seconds, nanoseconds // 1000, tzinfo=FixedOffsetTimezone(total_offset_minutes))`

In my experience, most of the interest in a `callproc`-type method (also `CallableStatement` in JDBC) is for handling output parameters from the stored procedure. In this case, we can use a bit of T-SQL code to retrieve those results. 

Example: For a stored procedure

```sql
CREATE PROCEDURE [dbo].[test_for_pyodbc] 
	@param_in nvarchar(max) = N'', 
	@param_out nvarchar(max) OUTPUT
AS
BEGIN
	SET NOCOUNT ON;

	-- set output parameter
	SELECT @param_out = N'You said "' + @param_in + N'".';
	
	--also return result sets
	SELECT N'SP result set 1, row 1' AS foo
	UNION ALL
	SELECT N'SP result set 1, row 2' AS foo;
	
	SELECT N'SP result set 2, row 1' AS bar
	UNION ALL
	SELECT N'SP result set 2, row 2' AS bar;
END
```

our Python code can do this

```python
conn = pyodbc.connect(conn_str)
crsr = conn.cursor()

sql = """\
DECLARE @out nvarchar(max);
EXEC [dbo].[test_for_pyodbc] @param_in = ?, @param_out = @out OUTPUT;
SELECT @out AS the_output;
"""
params = ("Burma!", )
crsr.execute(sql, params)
rows = crsr.fetchall()
while rows:
    print(rows)
    if crsr.nextset():
        rows = crsr.fetchall()
    else:
        rows = None
```

to produce this:

```none
[('SP result set 1, row 1', ), ('SP result set 1, row 2', )]
[('SP result set 2, row 1', ), ('SP result set 2, row 2', )]
[('You said "Burma!".', )]
```

Sure. I would just like to follow Semantic Versioning guidelines as closely as possible. While I don't think that technically is an API change, semver does say this:

> Minor version Y (x.Y.z | x > 0) MUST be incremented if new, backwards compatible functionality is introduced to the public API. It MUST be incremented if any public API functionality is marked as deprecated. **It MAY be incremented if substantial new functionality or improvements are introduced within the private code**. It MAY include patch level changes. Patch version MUST be reset to 0 when minor version is incremented.

Since there have indeed been quite a lot of changes since 0.3.0, I'd say 0.4.0 would indeed be a good choice.

While reading the Semantic Versioning text, I was reminded that major version 0 is special:

> Major version zero (0.y.z) is for initial development. Anything may change at any time. The public API should not be considered stable.

I guess we shouldn't wait too long to move to 1.0.0, as the API is in fact already pretty stable. Perhaps not the upcoming release, but the one following it. Or should we wait till CSL 1.0.1 is more fully implemented first?

autoenv automatically searches for .env files in parent directories: https://github.com/kennethreitz/autoenv/blob/master/activate.sh#L15

This allows you to nest the .env files at will.

Parent .env files execute so you can go into any directory. I'm a go developer, so I need to have GOPATH set. Assuming this structure:
```
myproject/
myproject/bin/
myproject/src/
myproject/src/awesome.go
myproject/.env
```
I can just cd to `myproject/src` and have my `GOPATH` set without needing to think about cd'ing to `myproject/`.

I hope this clears up the autoenv behaviour.
---
One thing that might worth changing is the .env file order. Currently, autoenv walks up to the mountpoint, and executes every .env file it finds, beginning with the bottom one. It might be wiser to do it in the opposite way so you can have generic variables/autoenvs/... in higher directories and project-specific variables in the project directory.

I have a big experience of working with all projects mentioned above and I think approaches used by these projects are fine (they are solving it's purpose). The only difference with gRPC is that they had implement response object on their own (except python-consul, they simply used dict for that :)) due to protocol nature (kazoo is using binary protocol on very low level and python-etcd and python-consul are working with JSON).

Our situation is better (I think), because `grpcio-tools` already did a lot of work for us and classes are available for usage. Some of them could be used as it is, others make sense to inherit (and extend) and only in a few cases we need to implement something new.

Hey, sorry for leaving in the old commits, should have started with a clean git checkout ;-)  Let me just talk through some of the other things that were buried in that PR.  If you find any of these interesting let me know and I'll clean up my git branch and do a new PR with individual commits for each feature, so you can cherry pick the ones you want.... would rather have you working on core cmdb functionality than fussing around with templates ^_^

* "Mem"  column renamed to "RAM [GB]" and conversion from MB corrected from  /1000 to /1024  (actually, I should probably use "[GiB]" to be clear)

+ "CPU Type" column with last element from the ansible_processor list (usually something like "Intel(R) Xeon(R) CPU E5-2651 v2 @ 1.80GHz"

* "CPUs" column changed to "vCPUs"  and uses the ansible_processor_vcpus  instead of ansible_processor_count (which on physical boxes seems to only provide physical socket count and not total CPU cores count).  vcpus still isn't ideal because it also counts HyperThreading as extra cores, but it's still better than characterizing our "Intel(R) Xeon(R) CPU E5-2620 0 @ 2.00GHz" as having "1" processor versus "12" (6 cores x2 for HT) 

+ "Memory usage" and "Swap usage" columns : yes, these really belong more in a systems monitoring tool like Icinga2, but ansible_facts provides good values for mem usage (that don't include cache+buffers which are essentially "free") so it'd be nice to have a sortable bar graph of them.  Plus, I often find this information critical in a CMDB for resource planning.  Use case:  managers are always asking "where can we save money in our cloud by decreasing the number of vCPU/RAM in our instances".   So having this info in the CMDB really helps us go back to them with "well, this app pool is using only 5% of their huge instances, while this other app pool is choking on their peasley little 2GB VMs"

* "Disk usage": added the "hidden sort helper" which puts the percentage fraction ( in the form: 0.000 - 1.000 ) in front of the cell contents so the JQuery properly sorts it by usage percentage, instead of alphabetically on the string e.g. "(6.87g / 8.06g)"  which doesn't always come out right.   This still isn't ideal, since it'll only sort by the usage of the first "/" partition, but it's an improvement.   In the future we could run an additional loop through all the mount points and use the max % utilized as the "hidden sort helper", but that'll take some more explaining.

This may not fix what your describing, since you're talking about the case of the actual value being passed, but you can map a function argument to a slot name with the action decorator's ``mapping`` parameter.

```
@ask.action('favorite-color', mapping={'color': 'Color'}) 
def echo_color(color):
    return statement('Your color is {}'.format(color))
```

For the case of the actual value, you should be able to determine that with how you define your custom slots. So,

COLORS
red
blue
green

Build time is O(n log n) because the memory usage of the tree is O(n log n). Even if we stored all of that data on disk and reloaded it directly, build time would still be O(n log n).

It may be possible to achieve some constant scale factor of improvement by storing all that memory, but then the on-disk storage size would be several times bigger, proportional to log n.

That's a different interpretation of intersection. The set operations implemented in this library on interval trees treat them simply as sets of interval objects, and those interval objects either compare equal or do not compare equal.

So if there are three unequal intervals, `a`, `b`, and `c` (no matter what their bounds are or if they overlap), then `IntervalTree([b, c]) & IntervalTree([b, a]) == IntervalTree([b])`

@MartinDelVecchio  Sorry for late reply, the first thing I noticed is the way you wrote your parameter definition:

```yaml
 parameters:
     - name: cls
       description:  Alarm class
       in: query
       schema:
         $ref: '#/definitions/AlarmClass'
```

The `Parameter.schema` can only be used when `in` equals `body`. You could rewrite your spec in this  way and try again.

```yaml
 parameters:
     - name: cls
       description:  Alarm class
       in: query
       type: string
       enum:
          - Network
          - Service
          - Storage
          - System
```

Refer to [here](https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/blob/master/versions/2.0.md#parameter-object) for details about `Parameter` object (it would be much consistent in OpenAPI 3.0.0) and please feel free to contact if it still goes wrong.

Sounds good. Thanks.

> 4. Are you relying on a set linesize? If the user sets it, would that be a problem?

Only partially. I want to check if a line that starts with a period is a code line and not an output line. I can't just do `code_line in result_line` because if `code_line` is longer than `linesize`, it won't work. So I currently check if the first `linesize - 5` characters of `code_line` are in `result_line`. By default, I have `linesize` set to 80, but that would be very easy to add to `%set`. 

I could also require fewer of the code characters to be present in `result_line`. If I had it set to `65`, then if linesize were reduced to 70 without the kernel's knowledge, everything would still work. Alternatively, widening the `linesize` without the kernel's knowledge is not a problem.

@teqie: the motivation for changing the library was mainly to leverage using Pillow to provide the drawing primitives, but the previous scrolling mechanism before was not very flexible - with the virtual viewport idea scrolling any direction is possible. It also means that any code written for this can work on an OLED or LCD display as well.

The rendering mechanism should be very fast - much faster than the previous version as this now uses hardware SPI to send to the MAX7219 - previously it was bitbanging in software. I benchmarked the library using an 8x8 LED matrix a few weeks back on a Pi Zero and it clocked about 260 FPS which is insanely fast. 

Just by the nature of how the MAX7219 chips are daisy-chained, any single pixel change will require the whole image representation to be flushed out to the device. That was true for the old library as it is for the new library. 

If you are seeing sweeping pixels, this implies you are force rendering after each pixel is updated, rather than rendering a full frame at a time. If that's just how your program works, there are ways around this - can you share your code? - I help can suggest ways to restructure it to make it perform better with the new library.

Oh awesome, I didn't realize you already had written code!

> And the field already is a MultiPolygon. It should be a MultiPolygon to support countries with islands, for example.

Right! And states like Michigan can only accurately be represented as MultiPolygons. I didn't realize Michigan also had islands as well.

> I would not worry too much about the native Polygon datatype (I did not see any reference to the native datatype in the project, perhaps I'm wrong).

The GIS contrib app distributed with Django (`django.contrib.gis`) has a `PolygonField` (see [here](https://django.readthedocs.io/en/stable/ref/contrib/gis/model-api.html#polygonfield)). Not that it matters since it sounds like `MultiPolygonField` is the better choice and you're already using it. 👍 

> > Do you want to support historical boundaries? If yes, I would suggest utilizing django-field-history on the boundary field. Doing so will automatically save a revision to modified boundaries every time the import command is run, and if the boundary isn't modified it won't save a revision.
> 
> I really don't think this belongs to the scope of this project. These boundaries rarely change and the users might care more about the most updated one, instead of a history of boundaries.

I agree, I only bring it up because I don't want to prevent users from implementing that themselves. I want this to be possible only by overriding one of our concrete models and configuring django-field-history instead of forking this project altogether. Hope that makes sense.

> As I said, I'm running into issues on how to run makemigrations. If you have any tips on that, let me know.

You might be able to use the management command in `test_project` to generate migrations for you, but you might have to set your `PYTHONPATH` environment variable first. In the root of your local django-cities repo, try this:

```bash
PYTHONPATH=. python test_project/manage.py makemigrations cities
```

and see if that works for you.

However, what I'm actually doing is using one of my Django projects to make the migrations for this app. It's a little complicated, but it works for me. My project directory structure looks like:

```
./project/settings.py  # Contains 'cities' in INSTALLED_APPS
./django-cities  # Git repository for django-cities, added to my project's ./.gitignore
./cities -> ./django-cities/cities  # symlink into the local dango-cities repo
./...other...stuff...
```

then I can run `python manage.py makemigrations cities` in my project root to have it generate the migrations in what it *thinks* is its `cities` app, when really it's creating them in the django-cities git repo. Double check those migrations for any dependencies outside of django-cities before commiting them to the local django-cities repo though!

* register with your flask app -> Bootstrap(app)
* restart the application

The flow you propose won't work. If resolving names is enabled, it will be done before HASS starts creating entities. HASS only works with the _finished_ pyhomematic objects, and they either have the IDs or the names from the CCU.
I can of course still enable compatibility with the entity registry. But this won't improve performance in regard to resolving names. Either all or none of the names are resolved prior the HASS creating its entities.
Another problem with the resolvenames option is when you add new devices to your CCU. As you said, entities get added to the registry automatically. From what I saw after looking quickly at it all it takes is a uniqe ID property. Using the ID that we already use as the entity ID would be perfect for this. But when resolving names and adding new devices, your device shows up in HASS instantly even before you had the change to assing a name on the CCU. So when it's first seen the ID is different from what you get after setting the name on the CCU.
I don't know what would happen if we just always set the uniqe ID to the one we get when not resolving names but still resolve names. There might be a conflict when the entity ID changes outside of the scope of the registry.
So in my opinion it would be no problem to enable the registry when resolving names is disabled. With enabled resolving we would need to test what happens and maybe find some workarounds.

If you generate a URL using django's template method thusly: 

`{% static '' %}` 

then the generated Google Cloud Storage path is 

`/<bucket-name>/.`

This is because the path after the /<bucket-name>/ section is passed through `clean_name()`.

Google Cloud Storage does not accept the generated path; it returns  a 302 to `/<bucket-name>/`. I've verified that hacking the `clean_name` function to strip the trailing `.` fixes the problem.

A rudimentary fix (based on my “quick-hack-fix above”) would be:

```
diff --git a/sqlalchemy_utils/types/timezone.py b/sqlalchemy_utils/types/timezone.py
index b474066..04bed07 100644
--- a/sqlalchemy_utils/types/timezone.py
+++ b/sqlalchemy_utils/types/timezone.py
@@ -37,12 +37,13 @@ class TimezoneType(types.TypeDecorator, ScalarCoercible):
         self.backend = backend
         if backend == 'dateutil':
             try:
-                from dateutil.tz import tzfile
+                from dateutil.tz import tzfile, UTC
                 from dateutil.zoneinfo import gettz
 
                 self.python_type = tzfile
                 self._to = gettz
                 self._from = lambda x: six.text_type(x._filename)
+                self._utc = UTC
 
             except ImportError:
                 raise ImproperlyConfigured(
@@ -52,12 +53,13 @@ class TimezoneType(types.TypeDecorator, ScalarCoercible):
 
         elif backend == 'pytz':
             try:
-                from pytz import timezone
+                from pytz import timezone, UTC
                 from pytz.tzinfo import BaseTzInfo
 
                 self.python_type = BaseTzInfo
                 self._to = timezone
                 self._from = six.text_type
+                self._utc = UTC
 
             except ImportError:
                 raise ImproperlyConfigured(
@@ -72,7 +74,7 @@ class TimezoneType(types.TypeDecorator, ScalarCoercible):
             )
 
     def _coerce(self, value):
-        if value is not None and not isinstance(value, self.python_type):
+        if value is not None and not isinstance(value, self.python_type) and not value is self._utc:
             obj = self._to(value)
             if obj is None:
                 raise ValueError("unknown time zone '%s'" % value)
```

+1 to having a `get_current_user()` or some such that returns the current object of the proxy -- I really don't understand the controversy.

I was just bit by this exact thing and found this issue, when using Flask-OAuthlib to store grant objects in a Redis cache. A grant must be created for the current user (and Flask-OAuthlib expects the user object on the grant, not just an identifier/username). But, just as evident by this discussion, I can't do `grant = Grant(user=current_user, ...)` and save that grant to Redis, because of this issue.

To be honest, I don't quite understand the reasoning behind having a `current_user` global proxy instead of putting the current user into Flask's `g`, which seems designed for stuff like this. Anyhow, that's a design decision that has long since been made, so OK. But why not make users lives easier by exposing a `get_current_user()` that doesn't affect this library's proxy usage, or existing code using this library, at all?

        pool.apply_async(dummy_handler, args='x')

def test_thread(x):
    for _ in range(x):
        threading.Thread(target=dummy_handler, args='x').start()

def timef(func, arg):
    start = time.time()
    func(arg)
    print "{0} took {1} seconds ({2} iterations)".format(func.__name__, time.time() - start, arg)


timef(test_pool, 1)
timef(test_thread, 1)

timef(test_pool, 10)
timef(test_thread, 10)

timef(test_pool, 1000)
timef(test_thread, 1000)

timef(test_pool, 100000)
timef(test_thread, 100000)
```

I was considering making an N-set diagram visualization, indeed, however this would require a more complicated general optimization procedure for laying out the circles and a completely different graphics engine instead of the ad-hoc circle intersections I am computing here (something based on `shapely` perhaps and supporting arbitrary Bezier curve intersections).

Exactly, it just so happens that both the admin and the generic views need have a method to get current language. Both methods had the same name and the same implementation, but they were architecturally completely unrelated.

Now, in hvad 1.3, I refactored the generic views so they follow the same conventions as Django's and so all edge cases would be clearly defined (there used to be some undefined behaviors). As part of that refactory, `_language` was renamed to `get_language`.
Deprecation path includes detecting the presence of the deprecated `_language` and raising a warning in hvad 1.3 and 1.4, then raising and AssertionError in hvad 1.5.

The admin side is a distinct module and was left untouched by this refactor, keeping the name `_language`. Merging admin and generic view mixins is completely unexpected, and pure chance leads to the behavior you saw: generic view's deprecation path complaining when it finds admin's internal methods, because it bears the same name as a deprecated generic view method.

Yep, I'd recommend you copy the needed code to your view. Perhaps create your own `LanguageTabsViewMixin` if you use it in several places. As you said, hvad is not an UI provider. It tries to be on par with Django on that matter: generic views, forms and admin. Plus Django REST framework support, which we believe is mandatory in modern websites.

It seems you are digging into hvad's internals in a very unexpected way. I don't really want to encourage this as it breaks the fundamental paradigm of hvad, that translations are for displaying only and do not take active part in the data model or business logic.

In your example, you attach translation suggestions to an object. It is not the same concept as an hvad translation. I understand they are really close and it feels intuitive to try and implement them by re-using hvad translations. Yet hvad would work the other way around: attach translations to suggestions.

I would suggest you split it into:
* the actual instance translations, managed by hvad, one per language.
* a separate model for suggestions (that you could use to help the user fill the actual translations)

Suggestions are part of your business logic, and pushing that into hvad's own data model risks creating incompatibilities with future versions.

I guess if you insist on doing this anyway, I could make it possible to override the `language_code` field definition. You could then add your own `language_code` definition in `TranslatedFields` and set it as you like, as long as you are aware of the caveats of messing with a third party module's internals.

Not coincidentally, it also makes it easier to write test code that gets coverage.

After adding a long running operation to the message handler, I get a `concurrent.futures.CancelledError` when the client disconnects. When the program stops, there is still a pending task: the writer. This is because `await self.close(wait=True, abort=True)` and `await self.queue.put(None)` were not called after the exception (`asyncio_socket.py`).

Example handler:
```python
@eio.on('message')
async def message(sid, data):
    await eio.send(sid, 'pong', binary=False)
    await asyncio.sleep(1)
```

The solution might be another except case around `await self.receive(pkt)`
```python
except concurrent.futures.CancelledError:
    break
```

Only suggestion...

change:
while self.status == u'in-progress':
            sleep(update_every_seconds)
            self.load()

to:
while self.status == u'in-progress':
            self.load()
            sleep(update_every_seconds)

Reason being, if a completion of say a power_on().wait() == True, the droplet may not actually be finished booting up, so putting the delay after load will give us that little extra wait time.
Thanks !

Hello Reynold,

this comment is related to the *getAllExecutionsResults* error messages:

reason for the *getAllExecutionsResults* errors is, that the TestLink V1.9.17 server itself has a known Bug [TL Mantis Ticket 8259](http://mantis.testlink.org/view.php?id=8259), documented inside [TestLink python API 0.8 CHANGES.rst](https://github.com/lczub/TestLink-API-Python-client/blob/v0.8.0/CHANGES.rst#known-tl-1917-issues )

I was unsure, if I should publish TestLink python API 0.8 supporting *getAllExecutionsResults* or not. But a fix is already publish inside the TestLink repository and you can easily implement it , by just extending inside _lib/api/xmlrpc/v1/xmlrpc.class.php_ the function _function initMethodYellowPages()_ with
`'tl.getAllExecutionsResults' => 'this:getAllExecutionsResults',` 

Alternative is, not to call *getAllExecutionsResults*, when using the published TestLink V1.9.17 version

Regards Luiko

With regards to how to perform gradient clipping with Gluon, another option is to specify gradient clipping as part of the `Optimizer` given to the `Trainer`. Although this will simply clip by value: potentially changing the direction of the gradient tensor and effecting different gradient tensors in different ways. So overall, `gluon.utils.clip_global_norm` is the best for maintaining direction and relative magnitudes of gradient tensors. Still, this is an example of how value gradient clipping can be done:

```
mxnet.gluon.Trainer(net.collect_params(), optimizer='sgd',
                    optimizer_params={'learning_rate': 0.1, 'clip_gradient':5},
                    kvstore='device') #for GPU
```

When elements of the gradient tensor are:
* less than -5, they will be set to -5, 
* greater than +5, they will be set to +5.

Hi @william-clark, Firstly, you need to create JIRAs, not ISSUES, since we are on Apache. So, you need to think in terms of a diamond. The BranchPythonOperator is `a` and the join condition `one success` is `d` below. `b` and `c` are the legs. You always need a leg. In some cases, you just need to add a dummy operator. a / \ b c \ / d To give you an example, here's mine : In my example, I branch at `check_for_time_to_build_model_branch_condition ` and join at `wait_for_previous_hour `. I need to add a single `prejoin_preagg_dummy_job`, which is just a dummy operator, right after the branch operator -- it is `c` in the diamond diagram above. The reason is that the first task after a branch is ALWAYS skipped. Hence, the one_success trigger rule is not evaluated. Just add a dummy operator

https://nodejs.org/api/net.html#net_server_listen_path_callback implies that the nodejs net/socket layer knows how to talk to named pipes if you pass in the correct path.  If that doesn't work, a simple file handle opened on the pipe path that `watchman get-sockname` returns should be usable instead.

We deliberately chose not to try to expose events at that level of granularity.  Unlike other watchers, watchman's primary purpose is to build a map of the current state of the filesystem and remember when each file was last changed.  This allows accelerating a common question asked by various tools.

To be able to provide consistent and conservative results in the face of either restarts or kernel notification queue overflows (where we have to re-crawl a directory) and because filesystem operations are fraught with TOCTOU race conditions, we track only that a file was changed (or possibly changed) and not the nature of the change itself.

In addition, different operating systems provide different levels of support for these various events that make it more complex to correct model and verify behavior.

It would be difficult to add support for this.  I'm not saying "no", but am questioning whether it is worth the time to do so.

What is your use case for wanting this additional level of granularity?

We use the date format for a couple reasons:

1. JSON schema can validate it
2. It's more read-able to people looking at the pulse stream or elsewhere without having to do the conversion.  

I can totally understand that there are two schools of thought on timestamps vs. date strings.  I personally prefer these read-able date formats, so I was good to go with it.  It feels a bit late to switch away from them now, tbh.  :|

I haven't tried this myself, but assuming you have your [ClusterComputeResource](https://code.vmware.com/apis/196/vsphere#/doc/vim.ClusterComputeResource.html) then you should be able to use the [configurationEx](https://code.vmware.com/apis/196/vsphere#/doc/vim.cluster.ConfigInfoEx.html) property (inherited from ComputeResource).
There is an array of group property in that, which is either [ClusterHostGroup](https://code.vmware.com/apis/196/vsphere#/doc/vim.cluster.HostGroup.html) or [ClusterVmGroup](https://code.vmware.com/apis/196/vsphere#/doc/vim.cluster.VmGroup.html). You can use that to find the vmGroup you are interested in, append your vm and call [ReconfigureEx](https://code.vmware.com/apis/196/vsphere#/doc/vim.ComputeResource.html#reconfigureEx).

E.g.
```
from pyVmomi import vim
from pyVim.task import WaitForTask
cluster # This is your cluster object
name # Name of the vmGroup
vm # The VM you want to add

for group in cluster.configurationEx.group:
   if group.name == name:
      group.vm.append(vm)
      spec = vim.cluster.ConfigSpecEx(groupSpec=[vim.cluster.GroupSpec(info=group)])
      WaitForTask(cluster.ReconfigureEx(spec=spec, modify=True))
      break
else:
   raise Exception("Failed to find group %s" % name)
```

With pyVmomi 6.0.0, you can use a parameter in the SmartConnect to specify a SSLContext which is then used. This context can be set to not verify the certificate.

A short code example as described in https://dellaert.org/2015/12/02/pyvmomi-6-0-0-vsphere-6-0-and-ssl/
```python
import ssl
 
from pyVim.connect import SmartConnect, Disconnect
from pyVmomi import vim, vmodl

# Disabling SSL certificate verification
context = ssl.SSLContext(ssl.PROTOCOL_TLSv1)
context.verify_mode = ssl.CERT_NONE
 
vc = None
 
vcenter_host = "10.0.0.10"
vcenter_port = 443
vcenter_username = "root"
vcenter_password = "vmware"
 
# Connecting to vCenter
try:
    vc = SmartConnect(host=vcenter_host, user=vcenter_username, pwd=vcenter_password, port=vcenter_port, sslContext=context)
 
except IOError as e:
    print "I/O error({0}): {1}".format(e.errno, e.strerror)
 
# Do stuff
```

MigrateVM only lets you change the host (and has an option to restrict based on state).
RelocateVM lets you change host, pool, datastore, disk types (thin <-> thick), network (standard switch -> distributed switch), leverage storage profiles and migrate cross VC.

RelocateVM pretty much does everything MigrateVM does, but much more.
I'd recommend using relocate and ignoring the existence of migrate.

The unit will have a buff if it is carrying minerals/vespene (See: Unit::buff_ids). It doesn't give you the value of the resource they are carrying, but there is a different buff_id for each type (eg. Normal vs High Yield minerals). You could hard-code an id to value map if you really needed to.

There isn't a robust way right now to tell which mineral field it is harvesting. When moving toward the mineral patch at least, it will be the "target_unit_tag" field of the order. That is not reliable as I think that target changes to the command center while it is returning the resources.

On the CommandCenter/Refinery there are fields Unit::assigned_harvesters and Unit::ideal_harvesters which tell you how many workers there are mining/collecting. That doesn't tell you which specific SCV units though as it is just a count.

There is another gotcha with vespene geysers. While the SCV is inside the geyser, the unit is not included in the ObservationRaw::Units. We have talked about possibly changing this to still include the unit.

I think the best practice would be to do like npm, and separate the two actions:

- `pipenv init` to create the virtualenv
- `pipenv install` to install

If you `pipenv install` outside of a virtualenv, we can give a warning, and look for near directory with linked virtualenv to offer as suggestion.

Another bonus with this approach is that it makes adding option to the virtualenv creation much more clean.

E.G: doing` pipenv install --python=version` is very weird.

You can also more easily add features that are specific to env creation, such as: https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/issues/1472

> the problem I'm facing is when I'm passing expiry time more than 7 days, minio-py throwing error (Expires param valid values are between 1 sec to 604800). so I'm not able to set greater expiry time than 7 days. AWS S3 allows any positive value as expiry time.
> Please explain why expiry time is restricted to 7 days only.

This is only true for AWS Signature V2, minio-py only implements Signature V4. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/API/sigv4-query-string-auth.html

`X-Amz-Expires` - Provides the time period, in seconds, for which the generated presigned URL is valid. For example, 86400 (24 hours). This value is an integer. The minimum value you can set is 1, and the maximum is 604800 (seven days). A presigned URL can be valid for a maximum of seven days because the signing key you use in signature calculation is valid for up to seven days.

I tested the bugfix branch with following result:
If a broker cannot establish a connection [here](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/bugfix/raise_connection_failures/pykafka/broker.py#L216), an exception is thrown. Therefore a `RequestHandler` is never being created and remains `None` for the current broker. But the exception is only logged [here](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/bugfix/raise_connection_failures/pykafka/broker.py#L101) and not raised again. For that reason the method `Cluster._request_metadata()` still tries to send a request at function [`broker.request_metadata(topics)`](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/bugfix/raise_connection_failures/pykafka/cluster.py#L249). But because the `RequestHandler` is `None` there will be an AttributeError [at this line](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/bugfix/raise_connection_failures/pykafka/broker.py#L327): `AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'request'`.
Maybe checking for `self._req_handler is None` [at line 327](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/bugfix/raise_connection_failures/pykafka/broker.py#L327) and raise a `SocketDisconnectedError` on `True` is a good option here instead of throwing a `AttributeError` to the main thread.

Hey, thanks for submitting this. You're totally right that we do need to provide a way of specifying the mandate PDF language.

However, I can't merge this as the code is auto-generated, and unfortunately the template is currently not open-source as it depends on some internal tooling (which we'd love to open source at some point).

Also, I'm not tremendously keen on using an environment variable for this, as it'd make it hard to change the language dynamically (e.g. if your GC integration was multi-lingual).

I think we'll probably end up allowing you to pass a dict of headers in:

```python
client.mandate_pdfs.create(params={...}, headers={'Accept-Language': 'fr-FR'})
```

Thanks again for submitting this! I'll update this ticket when we've got a solution.

Just throwing this out there:

We can rather simply detect if a given input is one form vs. the other. Instead of a complicated deprecation over years for a bad API decision, we could (I would argue should)

* detect and handle both types of input, emitting a warning for the old type
* detect which type it is, but only handle the new version. Raise an informative error if the old bbox format is used, telling the user how to fix the problem.

Again - I'm usually all for API stability but complicated deprecations guaranteeing multiple code changes are worse than just fixing the problem. In this kind of instance where we can reliably detect which is which, I strongly favor just making the change.

I didn't think there would be much difference in network overhead.  The request sizes are small and the response payload dwarfs it's envelope, which is also small.  Note that because the request message id and the arguments are the same, I *did* consider avoiding sending both, but I resisted, so far. :)

The main benefit is in pipelining the requests, which you'd get either way.

I suspect the main benefit of adding fetch to the protocol would be that it would reduce the number of future's created in the client, except that those futures provide value, as mentioned below.

A potentially-major advantage of using ``loadBefore`` is that responses can come back earlier. When fetching many ids, the client could start populating the cache for the earlier oids before the server has processed the later ones.  Along the same lines, application code blocking on the earlier requests could be unblocked sooner.

Finally, the strategy of having clients reuse futures for inflight requests relies on having a future per ``loadBefore``.

BTW, I did a test to simulate a bulk load, which would be roughly equivalent to adding the protocol, and prefetching and letting the application start loading objects right away was ~30% faster. And of course, the effect would be amplified on a slower (non-localhost) network.

Correct. Basically the front end will automatically refresh the token if it's been more than a minute or so since it's last been used, but after 10 minutes it can no longer be refreshed. So then it expires in another couple minutes after that.

> Because I am under the understanding that an infinate refresh window, will allow client to get a new token "forever"? and I can configure each of those issued tokens to have a lifetime of only 5 minutes for example?

Yes and yes.

The reasons people are against JWTs is mostly because it's not a good standard due to it being easy to implement incorrectly. This issue is a mess, there are multiple different issues all being piled into one place.

Some of these issues seem to be related to older Kivy versions; if you can duplicate these issues with the current version then please open a new issue.

As for this one: *C attributes cannot be added in implementation part of extension type defined in a pxd*

This is a known issue due to a bug in Cython. Kivy installs the .pxd files so that you can build your own code against Kivy's Cython code. But Cython checks the site packages path for .pxd files FIRST, and then checks the local folder. So if there is a conflict between the newer dev version and the older installed version, you will get this error. **The only workaround currently is to uninstall the currently installed version of Kivy, or make sure the installed version matches the version you are trying to build with buildozer.**

Perhaps a modified version of [cx_Freeze](https://github.com/anthony-tuininga/cx_Freeze) could be used?

With cx_Freeze, you tell it which modules are needed for your app (already in the kivy requirements), then it scans all those modules and finds their dependencies (you can add ones it can't find automatically) and packs ONLY the needed python modules and their dependencies into a single library.zip file. Also it renames all .so files (only from required modules / packages) in the format package.name.so and places them in a single folder.

```
build/library.zip # all data, py/pyc files
build/libx.so
build/libpython.so
build/my.pkg.so
etc...
```
Once this is done:

1. The library.zip could be extracted and packaged in the apk (unzipped). Since the apk is a zip, if the apk were added to the python path, the python files could then be imported directly with the standard zipimporter (maybe would need a modified path).
2. All the necessary .so files could be copied to the lib folder so they are extracted upon install.

Pros:

1. Automatically remove all unused modules (thus reducing overall footprint, i've seen 3x-4x reductions on desktop apps)
2. Save space of unpacked python assets (current cost is tar.gz size + unpacked size) as they would be loaded right from the apk (excluding .so files)
3. Remove startup time cost of unpacking assets on first run (since this would be done at install)

Cons:
1. Would need tested and implemented
2. Adds a startup loading time due to importing from zip (Importing only the stdlib.zip from crystax only takes 89ms to load)
3. Loader would need to know to import so files based on dotted name instead of path.

Edit:
I tested this by packing everything into a zip, putting the so files in a single location, hacking the import to load the correct so files, and the startup time was rather slow (extra 4 seconds) due to importing from the zipfile. So some alternative way needs investigated.

Perhaps extraction could be sped up using [czipfile](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/czipfile/1.0.0), or the library contents could be loaded into memfs or something.

shprint(sh.sed, '-i``` \'\'```', 's#^SSL=.*#SSL={}#'.format(openssl_build_dir), setup local)

NOTE: To clear up what needs to be **inserted** immediately following "-i" would be:

space backslash single-quote backslash single-quote

I'm not sure what I'm being asked to comment on.

The lsst_sims code that returns pixel coordinates for astrophysical objects currently returns those coordinates in the Camera coordinate system (in which the x axis is parallel to the serial readout direction/perpendicular to the serial register*).  This is accomplished by the `LSSTCameraWrapper` class defined here

https://github.com/lsst/sims_GalSimInterface/blob/master/python/lsst/sims/GalSimInterface/galSimCameraWrapper.py#L471

Does this answer the question at hand?
* I apologize if I got those backwards; I'm still acclimating myself to what all of those terms mean.  I do know that we are returning pixel coordinates in the Camera system, which is rotated 90 degrees from the DM system.

> In the scenario you describe, the user would have to set up the interpolation machinery separately at each location

I think you misunderstood me.  The user wouldn't have to do so, but rather they could call setupInterpolation after the shear call if that would be more efficient.  If you are doing a bunch of different achromatic shears, then you wouldn't want to do this.  Rather, you would want to setup the interpolation once and then apply the different shears to that.

But if you have a chromatic shear, then I think we still want to allow the user to setup an interpolation scheme after applying the shear to speed up the rendering.  That was really my point.

I think your proposed implementation may be a bit more complicated than necessary.  If there are transformations present when setupInterpolation is called, then the images it makes will include those transformations.  You can then reset `_A` and `_fluxFactor` back to an identity matrix and unity.  Any further (achromatic) transformations should then be applied to the interpolated images.

Applying a chromatic transformation can either be an error or it can remake the images by applying the transformation and redrawing.  I'm not sure which is better, since the latter is probably not the most accurate way to do things, especially if repeated several times, so it might be better to force the user to reorganize the code to do setupInterpolation after the transformations.

The DC1 depth problem was due to an error in the handedness convention of rotSkyPos.  That is related, but ultimately independent of whether we use the Camera coordinate system or the DM coordinate system for precise pixel positions.  The DM and Camera coordinate systems both agree on the direction of the z axis.  The DC1 depth problem arose because I had reversed the z axis in the CatSim coordinate transformation code.  Now that this has been fixed, we *could* go to the DM coordinate system without recreating that error.

Put another way: the problem we had in DC1 is that the code which determined which chip a given RA, Dec fell on was wrong.  The question of whether to use the Camera coordinate system or the DM coordinate system impacts the detailed pixel position of a given RA, Dec within the chip.  Both coordinate systems agree on which chip an RA, Dec position falls on.  They just disagree on the name of the exact pixel it falls on.

That being said, it's hard to argue with "if it ain't broke, don't fix it."  You are probably right.

Exactly, as you said:

> Having it listed in the `requirements.txt` means `pypandoc` will be installed whenever someone does `pip install ansible-tower-cli`.  I don't think that's the desired behavior.

This is the route of reasoning I followed. Putting it in the main `requirements.txt` file would be a bad idea. Say that we did want to implement some automatic conversion process. Ideally, that conversion would only be triggered when we are pushing a release. So, just like we have `tests/requirements.txt`, we could add a 3rd list of requirements. That would raise a lot of integration issues and it would probably be better to avoid that. Best just to leave it out altogether.

> My impression with your use of `pypandoc` was that it was a one-time thing used to convert existing markdown documents. 

Yes, because the output from pypandoc was a lot easier to read than my previous attempt. It seems reasonable to expect future contributors to maintain the README and HISTORY files from this PR. We are trying to strike a balance between that concern, getting the PyPI formatting right, and not breaking anything else. All of those considerations factored into the decisions made here.

I also had this issue. Looking at the code it seems its expected behaviour, although the error is super opaque. 

```python
class RestyResolver(Resolver):
    """
    Resolves endpoint functions using REST semantics (unless overridden by specifying operationId)
    """

    def __init__(self, default_module_name, collection_endpoint_name='search'):
        """
        :param default_module_name: Default module name for operations
        :type default_module_name: str
        """
        Resolver.__init__(self)
        self.default_module_name = default_module_name
        self.collection_endpoint_name = collection_endpoint_name
```

Basically if you do not pass the `collection_endpoint_name` kwarg to the RestyResolver call, it will assume that a general get on the collection endpoint maps to the `search` method. So basically you either add a search method, or pass the method you are using as a `collection_endpoint_name`.

There is a comment in the code that explains both of these issues:

        # add -f (show files in completion options) if there are arguments in the current section
        # in this case there are (usually) no subcommands to suggest, and only flags, so it's ok to suggest files,
        # and if the user types "-" first, then only the flags will be suggested

This means that if there are arguments, like <x>, then the only suggestion that can be offered is files (we don't want to add the string "<x>" to the command, this is usually not valid input). For flags, the user first has to type "-" and only then flags are offered as completion suggestions (if the next input is supposed to be an argument, <x>, we can't be sure the user wants to add flags at this point).

zsh's completion system is a little better, and there, in the case of arguments, the system will output the argument name ("<x>") as a message, but not as a completion suggestion, so it will not be completed into the command line. I put a little less effort into bash's completion system, which doesn't support this type of "messages" AFAIK.

Usage:                                                                                                     
  docopts [options] -h <msg> : [<argv>...]
    
Options:
  -h <msg>, --help=<msg>        The help message in docopt format.
                                If - is given, read the help message from
                                standard input.
                                If no argument is given, print docopts's own
                                help message and quit.
  -V <msg>, --version=<msg>     A version message.
                                If - is given, read the version message from
                                standard input.  If the help message is also
                                read from standard input, it is read first.
                                If no argument is given, print docopts's own
                                version message and quit.
  -O, --options-first           Disallow interspersing options and positional
                                arguments: all arguments starting from the
                                first one that does not begin with a dash will
                                be treated as positional arguments.
  -H, --no-help                 Don't handle --help and --version specially.
  -A <name>                     Export the arguments as a Bash 4.x associative
                                array called <name>.
  -s <str>, --separator=<str>   The string to use to separate the help message
                                from the version message when both are given
                                via standard input. [default: ----]
~~~
