TLDR; @jgeewax The changes you suggest in your last two comments just make `Entity` effectively a subclass of `Key` that also holds key->value data and index metadata.

The core thing I'm uncomfortable with is blurring the actual API concepts with our own. It makes the process of understanding the datastore API service difficult for our users.

------

RE: "The indexes piece will be changing." Is there some document to see? It's hard for me to imagine how our code should look based on an amorphous future.

RE: "Further, the index specification is rarely used" Evidence? It's not even possible to insert an indexed string > 500 chars or an indexed Entity in any form. So of course it's used plenty, because people store strings longer than 500 chars and store sub-entities. In addition people turn off indexing to avoid both cost ($) and slowdown (time) in writes.

RE: "an Entity is just the Key with data" I totally agree, but there are two types of data associated to the `Key`, the key->value pairs and the index metadata. This was part of the many discussions with Eli. (The index stuff may change, based on what you said, but I can't see the future from the present.)

RE: "A parent can be another Entity (that's how a lot of people think of it anyway..." Which people? That's a sign of mis-education of users. Not something we should help propagate.

RE: "it feels a bit backwards to create a Key that isn't really a Key -- it's a partial key... " This should be addressed with the API devs, not with the library that implements it. A Key can be incomplete.

In your second run, you'll notice that the compile of `//:MyApp` did not compile any sources (the "`Compiling X Scala sources to DIR`" message is not reported by zinc). So this is almost certainly an incremental compile bug in zinc. We're hoping to update zinc during the `1.4.0.dev` series (see #4477), which might address this issue and others.

Pants does not write incremental compiles to the buildcache by default (`--no-compile-zinc-incremental-caching` is set by default), for precisely this reason. So unless you enabled that option, the results of a incremental compile should not be stored there.

But you should always either 1) run `./pants clean-all` between CI builds, or 2) set `--no-compile-zinc-incremental` (note: different option). If you disable incremental compile entirely, you rule out these issues.

zinc incremental compiles are a deal with the devil which is really only safe to make on developer machines. Whether we should invert the default and disable it by default is an open question.

I'll start off a bit terse answering this and expect you to fire away with questions if any of this is unclear. Thanks for the detailed repro notes. I solidified them further in the 1st commit here: https://github.com/jsirois/issues-6398-repro

Pants has the concept of source roots talked about a bit here: https://www.pantsbuild.org/setup_repo.html#source-roots. These delineate where the normal package structure for the language at hand starts. For python, an autodetected source root is `src/python`. The idea is that that is the start of a PYTHONPATH; ie: it's where the package namespace root is. Put another way, for a small single python project in a traditional repository - thats where setup.py would go. As such, see the 2nd commit in https://github.com/jsirois/issues-6398-reprowhich aligns the test code with that reality and gets tests against src/python:: passing.

The reason standalone worked is because whenever a source root is not auto-detected, pants uses the parent directory of the BUILD file as that source root.

You could write your own "local" run iterator. That might be the best short-term solution. So something like:

```python
from pptx.oxml.ns import qn
from pptx.text.text import _Run

def iter_runs(paragraph):
    """
    Generate a _Run object for each run contained in *paragraph*, even if that run is enclosed in a
    `w:smartTag` element.
    """
    p = paragraph._p
    for element in p.getchildren():
        if element.tag = qn(`w:smartTag`):
            element = element.getchildren[0]
        if element.tag = qn(`w:r`):
            yield _Run(element)
"""

You might need to tune that up a bit, but general approach might work.

The other thing you can do is use XPath to get all the `w:r` descendants:

```python
r_elements = p.xpath('w:r')
for r in r_elements:
    yield _Run(r)
```

but that may return some runs you don't want (like those in deletion revision marks). This complexity is why it isn't in the library yet :)

It's also worth pointing out that several model types may trigger further feature elimination. The prime example are all decision trees and their ensembles (eg. random forests, gradient boosted trees) - if a feature is not involved in any tree splits, then it's also eliminated from the feature set (why compute a feature when it's not used by the prediction logic?).

The JPMML-Converter library, which underlies the JPMML-SkLearn library, inspects the (Lib)SVM data structure to identify no-op columns (pay attention to the `featureMask` local variable):
https://github.com/jpmml/jpmml-converter/blob/master/src/main/java/org/jpmml/converter/support_vector_machine/LibSVMUtil.java#L119-L194

Therefore, if you originally intended to retain 500 features, then the final PMML file may easily contain 350 to 400 features - the "missing" features were eliminated because the (Lib)SVM data structure indicated that they have no discriminative power.

Re @samzhang111 & @cathydeng's comments @AbeHandler: The longer the input string you're training on and trying to recognize the larger the computational space that your CRF needs to account for.  CRFs in so far as i understand their inner workings are essentially about aligning a list of possible tags on top of a list of input tokens.  So the longer the list of input tokens... the more possible alignments which have to be accounted for.  Ditto, for if there are a lot of possible tag arrangements (and combining lots of possible tag alignments with a lot of tokens could make things slow).  (One of the reasons i'm so impressed with DataMade is that they've found well defined problems to which CRFs could be applied, e.g. cleaning up names and chunking addresses into expected components.)

For the contracts... the question is whether you can break a full document or page up into sentence level chunks which'll be easier to classify and align tags on top of.  If there's a lot of boiler plate, and you can reliably and predictably detect and throw that junk out... just do that before you train or try to recognize the meaningful body content of each document.

Would have been clearer as "Since clean *may* be called before save". The point was that I couldn't just put the dataset inference logic directly in either `save` or `clean`, since I don't know which one is going to be called "first" (without the other having previously been called), and each one depends on the dataset being set. So that's why I put it in a separate `ensure_dataset` method that each of them calls. This also makes it easy to override (as illustrated) to define how the dataset should be inferred for a particular model.

In my experience, coming up with such regexps is often quite tricky due to the way that they work in Python (you have to anchor them at the beginning and at the end and some extra details that I forget right now).

@GlowingCrystallineEntity, can you open one bug report that only asks for this feature and we can make the discussion there more distilled?

Regarding the options with only one minus sign and more than one letter, I would love to kill those, since they seem to confuse argparse, which tries to interpret those as (possibly) two (or more) one-letter options being specified at once.

IMVHO, I think that we should only have long-options (double minus signs, like `--verbose`) or one-letter words, just like the utilities from the GNU project. Frequent options should have one-letter equivalents. Otherwise, only long-options.

I will open a new issue with this proposal, so that we can track this for the next (major?) release.

Hey @jaswilli ! Okay I've had another chance to go through these things, and think about the flow. This is actually an interesting use case because it's set up more like a protected server application (there is a web server inside a Docker image) that could easily run on a server and keep a client secret) but it's not, because the user generates it on the fly (and so we wouldn't save a client secret with it.)

> Native app clients should definitely be able to use "localhost" redirect URIs with either http or https. You don't even need to explicitly add them when configuring the client, you just get to use them automatically.

This is accurate - I retested the flow to use localhost as redirect uri, and it just worked. I'm honestly not sure why I was getting those errors the first time, but I think it's likely the many layers of clients I was using (more on this later).

> I'm not sure what all you have going on in the "workaround" version of your flask app but I suspect the invalid_grant error message is because you're somehow reusing the authorization code in a new "flow" and the OAuth2 PKCE check is failing.

I verified this by inspecting the globus client that makes the request vs. the globus client that is receiving it. What was happening is that in my init_globus_client I indeed was calling the function to initialize the authentication flow, and this is where the invalid grant came from, and it tricked me into thinking that the client state persisted.

> The native app grant is special in that to get around not having a client secret it generates one-time challenges per auth flow and that challenge gets associated with the auth code that's returned after a successful authentication. If you take a code and plug it into a new flow I suspect it will blow up on you.

This is what was happening. I think I had a few options here. I could try to stick my fingers into the globus python sdk and figure out where it's keeping those secrets, cache that, and then recreate the "same" client (I expect you wouldn't be in support of this) or I could do some hack in Flask or with a cache to try to save the same client. I didn't like this approach either because I can anticipate many bugs down the line with it, and looking at the code, it was getting weird and complex.

> Also, I noticed you're attaching data, specifically the globus sdk client instances, to Flask's app object. That data will persist across multiple requests so trying to do authorization flows with "global" state like that is going to be problematic--it may very well be the source of your problem. If you want to stash per-request data somewhere, check out g (from Flask import g).

I checked out g, but still had the same bug. I stepped back and decided to rethink this entirely.

**What I am working with**
 - The Singularity Global Client (sregistry) has a Globus plugin that is usable on the fly to work with a Globus endpoint. It does this via careful caching of tokens, and uses a Globus Native app client id. It works really well for command line because I can ask --> get --> retrieve refresh token on the first try, and then cache and be done. I don't need to worry about redirect and receiving the post.

**What I was trying to do**
 - The reason I was making it more complicated was to allow for tunel to use a different client id, which meant doing the flow over again, and sharing the same credential file.

**Why I was expecting it to work**
 - The Singularity Registry (server proper) uses an application with client secrets and this works totally fine, so I expected the same kind of flow to plug easily into a Native app implementation. I think it would per your sample app, but not easily in the weird setup I have with Flask and the client that is refreshed / reused being held by the server.

So here is what I did! I removed all the complexity by having the sregistry client used as it was intended. If the user tokens need to be updated (there is a function) they are shown this in the browser:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/814322/38514774-1dcd9306-3c00-11e8-8c23-fed32a75d978.png)

Then on the command line the update is running one command to the container, see complete details on that in development docs here:

https://vsoch.github.io/interface/plugin-globus#commands

The script itself is simple and stupid, as it should be. It calls a function using the same server client that checks if the tokens need an update, and then issues a request for endpoints that will handle the flow and provide the user with the url, etc.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python

from sregistry.main import get_client
import sys
...

    print('Updating Globus Client...')
    globus_client = get_client('globus://')
    if globus_client._tokens_need_update():
        globus_client._list_endpoints()
        print('Tokens updated!')
    else:
        print('Your tokens are up to date.')
```

And then the tokens are updated, we are using the sregistry client that the server already has installed without any weirdo stuff, and all is well until the user deletes the cache or otherwise mucks with it. Now when the user goes to the root globus portal in the tunel interface we have this working!

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/814322/38514178-6c770868-3bfe-11e8-9f3c-7f24d4f71059.png)

This should work well, and when/if someone complains about needing to use a command line I'll address this again then. This is also an edge case because the user is asked to do this step is done when the container is started (on the command line) and we would only hit this interface "your tokens need updating" very rarely.

Anyway, I'm happy with the fix, thanks for your insights!

>Maybe using a built-in event that any plugin could emit

I've thought about that but there's something about it that doesn't sit well with me. I think I'm not a fan of *raising an event* being used as the cause of something. Well, obviously events will always *cause* other things but if my **intend** is to do something - shutting down trinity - in this case, I feel like a method call would be more suitable.

In the initial plugin API, there's a stub for a `PluginContext` which is meant as an API that every plugin has access to. With that, one can think of a plugin calling something like `self.context.shutdown_trinity()`. That in turn could cause every plugin to receive an event (e.g. `TrinityShutdown`) that they may react upon. Alternatively, we could also imagine there wouldn't be an event because we can also just call  `cancel()` on every plugin to give them a formal way to cleanup.

With the new plugin API as described in https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/issues/1090 we could still think about injecting such a `PluginContext` or `PluginExternalAPI` or whatever one wants to call it, as part of `Plugin.__init__(args, api, token)`.

**Alternatively**, another more esoteric way could be to not *inject* a formal API into plugins but rather write another plugin to expose a common API. E.g. think of a `CommonAPIPlugin` that provides methods such as `shutdown_trinity()` (and what not) and that other plugins may use. The upside of this approach would be that a new API could be introduced without breaking the old one. As in: a newer API would just be yet another plugin that may even run side by side with the old one.

The downside of this is that it feels kinda weird and I think injecting *some sort* of API into plugins is rather fairly standard. ¯\_(ツ)_/¯

@carver @pipermerriam I tried to install toolz when build tools aren't available. Have a look at my [commit](https://github.com/ethereum/web3.py/commit/bffae8bfac363c02e5d0e8ddd502d458a4c423b2). 

However, this is not as simple as I thought it would be. Firstly, `cytoolz` is not the only package that `web3.py` uses that uses c extensions. `lru-dict` uses c extensions too. Moreover, things were fairly simple for switching  `cytoolz` --> `toolz` as both have similar module and API structure. The python equivalent for `lru-dict` is `pylru`, however their API's differ from each other. Besides packages like 
`eth-utils` also have `cytoolz` as a dependency.  If at all we had to make this switch, we'd have to make all other dependencies compatible too.

I don't think this is worth doing. Every time `web3.py` needs a dependency that uses c extension we'd have to explore a python equivalent package and write extra code for it just so that a windows user doesn't have to bother installing build tools?!?! And why compromise on speed offered by c extensions? Also `distutils` give a good enough error message upon failure:
```
error: Setup script exited with error: Microsoft Visual C++ 14.0 is required. Get it with "Microsoft Visual C++ Build Tools": http://landinghub.visualstudio.com/visual-cpp-build-tools
```
Also, ubuntu users have to install `python-dev` tools whenever they use c extensions:
```
sudo apt-get install python3.6-dev
```

> AFAIK, conda doesn't care about the state of your system on disk. It only cares about the current transaction. So you can install conflicts as long as you do it in two different conda "transactions". (at least this was true). For example, if you installed ipython and then later installed an incompatible version of pyzmq (pretend it is 0.0.1), you wouldn't know you broke ipython. 

Since tone doesn't come across short comments:  this isn't meant to be a value judgement against conda.  We have actively chosen to implement conda in this simpler manner.  I wanted to remind others of this detail.  Something like rpm has a local database of state and will prevent you from installing things that conflict with previously installed packages.

Furthermore: the way the code currently works, hard linking is always preferred, regardless of settings. It is impossible to avoid hard linking when the filesystem allows it. Soft linking is just an optional fallback when hard linking fails.
This is solved by `conda clean --packages` and then `conda update conda`.

Oh, I just realized why you might be led to believe this is working. It will finish _installing_ the packages---but it will mark them all as coming from the channel "<unknown>", because the channel information was missing. The fix is that the conda-env post-link script has been removed.  It has been replaced by a modified Lib/site.py file in each Python installation, plus a library_bin.pth file in each Python installation.  The combination of these lead to Library/bin being added to os.environ["PATH"] for any Python process.  Any programs you install that end up in Library/bin (for example, patch) will still not be on PATH for older installations.  You can either modify PATH to add Library/bin yourself, or uninstall and reinstall using a new installer (Anaconda >= 2.4.0; Miniconda >= 3.18)

Regarding runner_cfg
==============
The `runner_cfg` is a dictionary with the following fields:
-  `"enabled_test_cases"`
   - A list of test case names to run or "\_\_all\_\_" meaning all test cases
- `"output path"`
  - The path to the output directory for the test. Each individual test gets a separate output directory.
- `"active python runner"`
  - Always True when running using external automation.
- `"tb path"`
  - The path to the folder containing the test bench.
*NOTE:* Note the space in some of the dictionary keys. I know it is weird and we should probably change that.

On the branch there is also a `"use_color"` field to enable or disable ANSI color output.
This is set to true in the terminal and false in the simulator GUI:s that do not handle ANSI color codes (which is all of them).

Regarding test results
==============
The [VHDL runner](https://github.com/VUnit/vunit/blob/master/vunit/vhdl/core/src/core_pkg.vhd) places a file named `vunit_results` in the test output path. For each started test it prints `test_start:<Test name>` and when `test_runner_cleanup` has been successfully run it writes `test_suite_done`. If the simulation ends without `test_suite_done` the latest started test is failed.

General thoughts
===========
In a VUnit `run.py` file users can normally set compile options and simulation options. They can also configure the same test bench to run for multiple generic values. If you do not use the public API and also your own simulator abstraction this will not be available to you. VUnit also supports comment pragmas that can be placed in the test bench such as `fail_on_warning` `run_all_in_same_sim`. In the future we might add more pragmas for configuring runs with different generics and simulation options as well as compile options. Depending on you integrate this or the perspective of your users they might find some "normal" features of VUnit broken or missing in your environment.

It would be easier for you and more stable if you can re-use https://github.com/VUnit/vunit/blob/master/vunit/test_bench_list.py and https://github.com/VUnit/vunit/blob/master/vunit/test_bench.py.
These classes parse the test bench files for tests and pragmas and creates the test suite classes where the `runner_cfg` creation and `vunit_results` parsing is done.

You just have to pass it your own `simulator_if` instance that has a `simulate` method that conforms with the API used by VUnit.

There is no "splitting" of arguments going on between VUnit and your wrapper script. It is only when issuing a command in a shell where a tokenization of the input string into a list of strings (argv) occurs.
When invoking a subprocess from a program in for example C or Python it is normal to invoke the executable directly without having a shell in the middle tokenizing a string by setting the argv directly. This is the most exact and precise method to provide the argv list of strings to the subprocess when calling it. If your wrapper script was itself a Python or C-program it would be trivial for that to in turn start GHDL as a subprocess using the exact same and unmodified argv. Anyway doing it from a shell script seems less obvious (More reason to avoid shell scripts in favor of real programming languages) but after googling I found the "$@" bash variable which seems to solve this problem.

```
ghdl "$@"
```

Edit the file `/var/lib/dpkg/info/elasticsearch-curator.postrm`:

Change the following line:
```  rmdir /opt/elasticsearch-curator```

Replace by this one (force delete):
```rm -rf /opt/elasticsearch-curator```

Run the install again:
```sudo aptitude install elasticsearch-curator```

es = elasticsearch.Elasticsearch([es_server])

script_path = os.path.realpath(__file__)
working_path = os.path.split(script_path)[0]

ES_TYPE = 'data_feed'
ES_INDEX = 'l'


def main():
    if sys.argv[1]:
        path_file = os.path.join(working_path, sys.argv[1])

        if os.path.isfile(path_file):

            if os.path.splitext(path_file)[1] == '.json':
                with open(path_file, 'r') as f:
                    data = json.load(f)

                print(data)

                print('Importing:', path_file, '(', int(os.stat(path_file).st_size / 1024 / 1024), 'MB', ')')
                es.index(index=ES_INDEX, doc_type=ES_TYPE, body=data)

    else:

if __name__ == "__main__":

For` PageBlobService` and `AppendBlobService`, `put_blob` was replaced with `create_blob`.

You can refer to [Breaking Changes](https://github.com/Azure/azure-storage-python/blob/bc985392407459717634b46eda7d8f66b7ffb4dc/BreakingChanges.md)  to see a more detailed list of the API changes.

At any rate,  `//` in  `has//double//slashes` corresponds to an empty directory and on our end these values also appear to be preserved with both create and fetch operations.

Hi @Ashokcs94,

1. the chunk size is set using the variable MAX_BLOCK_SIZE on BlockBlobService, by default it is 4MB. You can set it to anything under 100MB.

2. create_blob_from_path chunks up your file and upload the chunks using put_block, and then do a put_block_list to commit all the blocks. You do not need to commit the blocks yourself.

3. get_block_list returns you 2 lists:
- Committed Block List:
    - The list of blocks that have been successfully committed to a given blob with Put Block List.
- Uncommitted Block List:
    - The list of blocks that have been uploaded for a blob using Put Block, but that have not yet been committed. These blocks are stored in Azure in association with a blob, but do not yet form part of the blob.

Please refer to [the doc](http://azure-storage.readthedocs.io/en/latest/ref/azure.storage.blob.blockblobservice.html#azure.storage.blob.blockblobservice.BlockBlobService.get_block_list) for more info. 😄 

Best regards,

Ze

I'll respond to your comments about the background first. Among the many use cases of bullet, consider the following two (note that I'm not judging or advocating for either one, just listing them as different types of considerations):

* Monolithic applications that uses bullet and have no downstream developers writing code that links against it (such as games, visual effects software)
* Modular libraries that use bullet and have downstream developers writing code against it (such as the [gazebo robotic simulator](http://gazebosim.org), of which I am a core developer)

I think static linking is fine for monolithic applications, but it causes problems for use in software stacks involving more modularity. When you have multiple pieces of software interacting on a system, there can be problems if different pieces are using different versions of the same library (most prominent in my mind are some difficult linking problems).

Regarding the different behavior of each version, we use [pkg-config in cmake](https://bitbucket.org/osrf/gazebo/src/default/cmake/SearchForStuff.cmake#cl-361) with logic to prefer specific versions of bullet. For example, you fixed some bugs in version 2.81, so we have been requiring version 2.82 or greater for gazebo.

Regarding your specific questions, a source install will have a default `CMAKE_PREFIX_PATH` of `/usr/local`, so the `btVector3.h` header would be installed to:
~~~
/usr/local/include/bullet/LinearMath/btVector3.h
~~~
When packaged as a binary for debian systems, the prefix is typically `/usr`, so it would go to:
~~~
/usr/include/bullet/LinearMath/btVector3.h
~~~
Using [homebrew](http://brew.sh) on Mac OS X, the prefix is usually `/usr/local/Cellar/bullet/version`, so version 2.82 would be installed to:
~~~
/usr/local/Cellar/bullet/2.82/include/bullet/LinearMath/btVector3.h
~~~
but homebrew also makes sym-links to `/usr/local` so it would also be available from
~~~
/usr/local/include/bullet/LinearMath/btVector3.h
~~~

As a downstream developer, I can use `pkg-config` or cmake `find_package` to get info about where the header files are installed. The pkg-config file bullet.pc is configure from the template [bullet.pc.cmake](https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/blob/master/bullet.pc.cmake) to add a `-I` argument to the `CFLAGS` variable that resolves to something like the following:
~~~
-I/usr/include/bullet
~~~
With that include flag, I can use the following:
~~~
#include <LinearMath/btVector3.h>
~~~
If we add another `-I` argument:
~~~
-I/usr/include/bullet -I/usr/include
~~~
then the following would work as well (which I prefer):
~~~
#include <bullet/LinearMath/btVector3.h>
~~~

Thanks for reply. aiohttp does provide Last-Modified header and it doesn't have Etag support yet. So If-Match and If-None-Match handler isn't necessary as clients won't make requests with them. (They won't get any ETag beforehand)
ETag is better than Last-Modified on these occasions:
1. a file whose last-modified is changed a lot without changing its content.
2. a file with its content being changed serveral times in a single second. (minimum unit for Last-Modified header is sec)
3. you cannot get the accurate last-modified from a file
Usually we don't change static resources a lot. And generating strong ETags requires some caching and hashing work. It's not cheap. Especially when dealing with large files.

Background:

CORS severely limits what you can do from one site to another. Prior to making a CORS request, browsers make an OPTIONS request, to ask the server what should be allowed. One of the things that is limited is setting headers on those requests. The server specifies which headers should be permitted with Access-Control-Allow-Headers.

Since part of the point of the token authorization is to make authenticated cross-origin access *easier*, we should allow the Authorization header to be included, which is what this PR does.

Following on from my comment on the issue (https://github.com/pylast/pylast/issues/180#issuecomment-253419535), I'm not sure whether to remove missing methods.

First, pylast supports both Last.fm and Libre.fm (and any other compatible APIs). If we remove missing Last.fm methods, we need to be sure that Libre.fm don't and aren't about to support them.

Also, it'd be relatively straightforward to remove missing Last.fm methods, but the lack of communication from Last.fm over this big mess means we don't know if or when these methods are returning. They may well be, as some have been reinstated. Whereas getting an error makes it obvious something is missing, it'd be harder to find out when they are reinstated and revert the code removal.

So it may be better just to leave them in and have users of pylast handle the exception (like the [unit tests](https://github.com/pylast/pylast/blob/develop/tests/test_pylast.py#L33-L46)). Perhaps some extra warnings could be shown making the situation clear and asking them to report the problem to Last.fm at https://getsatisfaction.com/lastfm.

Then again, it's been 14 months since they broke it...

Yes, because transferring tests and fixtures over the wire (between workers and the master node) is quite difficult/impossible, so every worker collects all tests and the master just schedules which test to run based on their node id (take a look at [OVERVIEW](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest-xdist/blob/master/OVERVIEW.md) for more details).

Some related issues: #270, #271

At a high level it goes somewhat like this: each worker process needs to run the collection themselves and they all report the collected tests to the primary.  This is when the primary checks they are all the same (I'm not sure on the implementation details here.  Once all workers have collected their tests the primary gives them a block of tests to run, they run the block of tests and report back at which point they get the next block etc until all tests are run.

I haven't looked at the implementation for a while but I suspect there is probably reason that the obvious-to-us set-comparison is not used.  But it's probably worth investigating if it could be improved.

Hi Laszlo,
Your configuration for slot 0 as a private key with ECDH enabled looks fine. There are a number of other reasons why this might fail:

1. Data zone must be locked for the ECDH command. You already mentioned this, but I wanted to clarify that the OTP zone isn't locked separately from the data zone. It would be better labeled as the data/otp lock.
2. Slot 0 must have a valid private key in it. This is done by running GenKey with Mode=0x04 and KeyId=0x0000, which internally generates a new random private key for slot 0. Since your configuration disables GenKey after the data zone is locked, this must be run while the data zone is unlocked to initialize the private key.
3. The public key must be a valid point on the P-256 curve. An improperly formatted public key or or one not on the P-256 curve may cause an execution error. Public keys will be the X and Y integers as big-endian, fixed-width, unsigned integers concatenated together. I've included a valid public key below for testing.
```c
uint8_t public_key[64] = {
    0xb2, 0xbe, 0x34, 0x5a, 0xd7, 0x89, 0x93, 0x83,
    0xa9, 0xaa, 0xb4, 0xfb, 0x96, 0x8b, 0x1c, 0x78,
    0x35, 0xcb, 0x2c, 0xd4, 0x2c, 0x7e, 0x97, 0xc2,
    0x6f, 0x85, 0xdf, 0x8e, 0x20, 0x1f, 0x3b, 0xe8,
    0xa8, 0x29, 0x83, 0xf0, 0xa1, 0x1d, 0x6f, 0xf3,
    0x1d, 0x66, 0xce, 0x99, 0x32, 0x46, 0x6f, 0x0f,
    0x2c, 0xca, 0x21, 0xef, 0x96, 0xbe, 0xc9, 0xce,
    0x23, 0x5b, 0x3d, 0x87, 0xb0, 0xf8, 0xfa, 0x9e
};
```

Could you try using the `transacted_postgresql_db` fixture instead? It's actually quite a bit faster if you're not doing anything too special. (More info on what I mean [here](http://pytest-pgsql.readthedocs.io/en/latest/#which-do-i-use).)

Anyway, I've found the problem. The issue is that we use SQLAlchemy to issue the DDL for creating tables in `create_table()`, but we drop them manually to avoid the overhead of reflecting SQLAlchemy models back from the database to drop them.

Since SQLAlchemy doesn't quote identifiers if it doesn't need to, the DDL looks like

```sql
CREATE TABLE Device (_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY);
```

Postgres lowercases unquoted identifiers behind our back so the table created is actually named `device`.

In the cleanup phase, we drop the tables ourselves, so the statement issued is

```sql
DROP TABLE "Device" CASCADE;
```

Here the name is quoted, so Postgres looks for a table named `Device` but doesn't find one.

You need to call poll() to trigger the delivery_callback of delivered message.
You already do this after produce() which is good, but will only serve delivery reports of previously produced messages, so if you have periods where you are not producing messages you should call poll() say every 1s or so.
flush() will wait for all outstanding messages to be delivered, so it is generally only used on termination to make sure all messages are produced or failed before exiting.

I'm not sure that "throw something at the parser and see if it's a date" is really going to be *terribly* fruitful, because the parser tries to err *way* on the side of false positives - if it sees anything that could reasonably be a date, it will give you a datetime.

That said, I think the problem here is that these strings are made entirely of valid separators and valid values for date *or* time, but we're not keeping track of which ones get used (or possibly as it walks through the string it finds new values and silently overwrites the original ones it picked up), and the fact that the separators don't actually *separate* anything is not being picked up. I imagine this will partly get solved when we start enforcing that we shouldn't find two answers for the same value, but if that's the problem, why is the "3" in `4.3` getting ignored? It's certainly not getting overwritten by anything later..

@burningTyger @ssbanerje actually I figure out that for some reason tmux no longer execute the ```source``` command inside its ~/.tmux.conf at startup anymore. So to fix this problem, you can simply remove (comment out) the ```source``` command from the ~/.tmux.conf altogether:

```tmux
# source "/usr/lib/python3.4/site-packages/powerline/bindings/tmux/powerline.conf"
```

and add the following lines to anywhere in your ```.zshrc``` (or ```.bashrc```):

```zsh
if [[ -f /usr/lib/python3.4/site-packages/powerline/bindings/tmux/powerline.conf ]]; then
    tmux source "/usr/lib/python3.4/site-packages/powerline/bindings/tmux/powerline.conf"
fi
```

Oops, I forgot to handle if there isn't any negative item. All the tests should pass now. I don't know why `tests/test_movielens.py::test_logistic_precision` failed on CircleCI only. I didn't even touch the code of logistic loss.

I've tried to train using my own dataset. The precision and NDCG scores were essentially the same as those of the original implementation.

Basically, my changes follow the original papers of WARP and WARP k-OS.

In the paper a random negative sample is picked from the set of negative items, while LightFM samples it from the whole items: `negative_item_id = (rand_r(&random_states[{thread_num}]) % item_features.rows)`. It can be implemented this way as long as the positive item picked is not counted as a "wrong" sample, i.e. `sampled` is not incremented when the positive item is picked.

The second change I made is regarding the loss computation. In WARP paper the numerator is <number of labels/items> - 1 because there is only one true label. If there are more than one label, it should be <number of labels/items> - <number of negative items> as proposed in WARP k-OS paper.

The three metrics you mention measure different things, so it's perfectly natural for a successful model to have different AUC, precision, MRR, and recall scores. I would look at their Wikipedia definitions to get a feel for how to interpret them; for what it's worth, your models' scores look fine!

Hey,
I'm a bit disappointed that you essentially want to ignore 90% of the features of sacred. :sweat_smile: 
But here's what you could do: Since you only want to use the logging of sacred you can just use the `Run` and not the experiment. You would have to manually create a Run instance. Something like this (untested):
```python
from sacred.run import Run
from sacred.utils import create_basic_stream_logger
from sacred.observers import MongoObserver

config = {'foo': 10}
root_logger = create_basic_stream_logger
observers = [MongoObserver.create()]
experiment_info = {}
host_info = {}

def main_function(foo, bar=2):
    return foo*bar

r = Run(config, None, main_function, observers,
                 root_logger, run_logger, experiment_info, host_info,
                 [], [])

# And then run it:
result = r(config['foo'], 7)
```

This doesn't use any of the automatic argument filling or reproducibility, just the saving the results to the database.

At first it did not look like a leak, but let's see:

- The memory is allocated inside the methods that print to a stream. 
- The stream object is a local variable inside of parse_smtlib_string in api_parsers. 
- When there is an error, which is the case here, a string is extracted from the buffer and stored in an error buffer (std::string).
- This gets printed.
The reported leak is for some facility that extends a stream buffer (std::ostringstream).

The particular behavior of C callers in the c_example is that they set a longjump when an error is set inside the API methods. So this could explain the phenomenon: the destructor on the stack allocated ostringstream is not called and therefore the memory on that object is leaked.

Yes. The implementation in Lasagne is a pure Theano implementation, based largely on the one from pylearn2. It basiaclly does a for loop over each of the values within a normalization window.

There is a wrapper for the local response normalization layer from cuda-convnet in pylearn2, but we haven't wrapped it in Lasagne afaik.

The main reason this layer class is provided is for "historical purposes". Local response normalization is obsolete (even when Krizhevsky used it, it only affected the classification accuracy by very little). So we are unlikely to spend time to optimize this. My recommendation would be, don't use it unless you have a very good reason to.

> Is ipyvega obsolete?

No, it's the main supported way to display vega & vega-lite in the classic notebook, and is used by altair for that purpose.

> Are Altair, ipyvega, and maybe Vincent developed by more or less the same group of people 

Vincent was developed by a different group, and I'm not certain about it's status. Altair and ipyvega are more or less developed by a common group of people.

> and each is a successor to the last?

No. The purpose of ipyvega is to display vega & vega-lite plots in the classic notebook. The purpose of Altair is to provide a Python API to generate vega & vega-lite charts, and due to constraints in how charts are displayed among various frontends, it was cleaner to put the general display architecture for vega & vega-lite within Altair, while still depending on ipyvega for display in the legacy notebook.

That's why I recommended you depend on Altair if you want a general display solution for vega & vega-lite plots.

Hope that's clear.

There are some problems with current DDG generation, what needs to be solved:

- These two SimStackVariables have the same hash: `<Stack sp+0x4, 4 bytes>`, `<Stack bp+0x8, 4 bytes>`, which can make digraph edge iterations inconsistent, see https://github.com/networkx/networkx/issues/2282

- Normalization and keep-state are not consistent in the current version: https://github.com/angr/angr/issues/223

- Other ones mentioned in this thread.

As far as I know, @ltfish is working on some improvements, which probably will affect DDG generation too. 

I've shared the code I've done so far, but need to warn you, it is in a pretty messy state currently.

You can check it here: https://github.com/axt/angr-ti

CFG and DDG visualization is depending on https://github.com/axt/bingraphvis, you need to install that, or remove the calls to graph generation.

`_index_cache` contains the information from the lazy-loading so just some basic data like the id, backend type etc.

`_data` contains the actual data for each schema attribute. So for  a `Job`'s `id` attribute, `Job.id` is a descriptor which stores and retrieves its data from `j._data`. `_data` should only.

While a job is only lazy-loaded, the `_index_cache` dict should contain information but `_data` should be empty. Once the job is fully-loaded, `_index_cache` should be empty and `_data` should be filled. The object instance's `__dict__` should contain only things like `_data`, `_index_cache`, `_parent`, `_proxy_object` etc. and not any of the schema data directly.

I asked on IRC in #postgresql and they said that:
- The INSERT itself optimistically looks for the row in the index and if it finds one is already there, it doesn't try to actually write.
- Locking a row does a write (because the lock is stored in the row itself as part of the t_xmax field).

So probably we can't do much better than this except by going back to a SELECT first. I don't think doing it in the same query is really that much better than doing it as two separate queries. I'll write up a PR.

Please note that the purpose of this project is to provide the best integration of our hardware, and not any sophisticated algorithms. In today's CV ecosystem, there are so many great solutions for these types of problems, some open source, some not, some cross platform, some not. 
The most natural way to approach this problem is to detect faces in 2D via OpenCV ([using Haar Cascades](https://docs.opencv.org/3.3.1/d7/d8b/tutorial_py_face_detection.html)).
Once you have the bounding box for the face, you'd need a classifier that could distinguish between a face and a fake. The simplest one can be plane-fitting and looking at depth variance. More reasonable approach is to train a deep-learning classifier with some fake and some real data. 
While this classification problem is hard in 2D, with depth your neural network will instantly pick up on the differences.

`points` frame behaves like the depth frame it was created from: It contains the array of stacked rows.

I just ran into this myself. After some source-diving and doc perusing I've more or less discovered how it works: the query must be in "long form" where it starts with `query` followed by an operation name and typed argument list. As far as I can tell this operation name is completely arbitrary (someone please correct me if I'm wrong).

``` 
query someMadeUpString(thingId: String!) 
{ 
  getById(id: $thingId) { someField someOtherField }
} 
```

You can then provide a binding to `thingId` by passing it in under `variable_values` to `.execute`:
```
schema.execute(query_string, variable_values=dict(thingId="123"))
```

> I just tried a user change page, as mentioned in the PR, and for me, after a long time, my local server gives a 2,159,708,387-byte response, and the page fails to load. That's just over 2 GB! 

I'll confess, that's not a metric I even considered, and is ... let's say ... unacceptable.

> Another option could be to do something like the SQL panel, where it detects duplicate templates or contexts and doesn't bother to render them, instead noting by the template path that it was duplicated X times.

That's an interesting idea, because even in the test page I was using, the sheer number of templates/repetitions was overwhelming to the point of potential uselessness.

> Alternatively, if it is possible to retrieve template contexts once the page has already rendered, perhaps contexts don't load until you click on them.

This was something I thought about doing, as one of the other panels does something similar, but I thought you might end up losing context debugging if a number of requests go through that exceeds `RESULTS_CACHE_SIZE` ... which would also prove useless and frustrating.

In my personal opinion:
- there probably does need to be an `TEMPLATE_IGNORE_PATHS` (or whatever) option, at the very least as an escape hatch for if nothing ele can be done.
- `SHOW_TEMPLATE_CONTEXT` should probably also short-circuit the whole context-walking part of the function, rather than just the display of it at render. It won't be a huge boost, but it makes sense to avoid the work we can, given there's now so much more to potentially do.
- Collapsing duplicates is interesting, and worth exploring. Possibly some JS to copy a context from the original node to the duplicate on the client-side would make the most sense, rather than merging templates, so that the template list is still a linear representation? Not sure.

addendum: `'django/forms/widgets/attrs.html'` and `'django/forms/widgets/select_option.html'` were responsible for a lot of the churn in my testing, too. So at least there's some consistency :)

1. I agree that such workaround is possible as it would be the least disturbing change.
2. I'm not quite sure I follow how this would help to get from a static timer handler context into the class context without a similar mechanism to 1.

--- OFF-TOPIC ---
As a general thought, some things about the Timer do not feel right. First the dependency on the otInstance (with a separate timer scheduler) and then another dependency on a Context, which on one hand (single instance case) has no context and on another has multiple contexts. This comes from my personal view that a timer is (should) be a simple special-case system event which calls your handler with a provided context. I would like to see otTimer API, where an application could use OpenThread timers to schedule it's own events OR the whole Timer, TimerScheduler and Alarm reduced to one static (i.e. single instance) platform-provided timer API. This way there wouldn't be a need for duplicate Timer scheduler implementations - OpenThread timer schedulers for networking and OS or platform timer scheduler for the application. Pros for this are reduced program memory due to duplicate scheduler implementations and simplified code (a lot less GetOwner), which would even further reduce program memory footprint. Cons - like you've mentioned increased RAM usage. I wonder if there's a way to get the best of two.
--- ON-TOPIC---

Hi all,

At prediction time, fastText ignores empty lines and outputs `n/a` for such lines. A line is considered as empty either because it does not contain any words, or because all the words are out-of-vocabulary (i.e. words that were not in the training set or seen less than `–minCount`).

A potential solution not to have empty lines is to add a "dummy" word to all lines, such as `</s>`.  This can easily be done with the awk command line tool:
```
awk '{print $0" </s>"}' train.txt
awk '{print $0" </s>"}' test.txt
```

We will change this behavior in the future.

Agree that @space88man has described the expected behavior, and therefore we have a bug. Changed the tag (question -> bug). Had a quick look at the code, won't be a super easy fix, but definitely doable. Also super glad we have very good unit tests on this part of our code.

Here's the current steps for parsing user input:

1. get user input
2. strip comments
3. expand shortcuts and aliases
4. use `shlex` to split on whitespace (this is where the error found by @space88man is coming from)
5. do additional word splitting to account for output redirection, etc.
6. look for command terminators
7. look for output redirection and piping
8. check if the command is multiline, if it is and if we don't have a terminator, go back to step 1 to get more user input.

To fix this bug, we need to do step 8 before we run anything through `shlex`.

py::array_t<double> get_array();

Perhaps a better approach, however, is to move some of this error handling logic around. After the `labels = domain.split('.')` line, we can check if the entire domain is too short or long. If the domain is empty, raise an error saying so. You can move the `if len(domain) > 255` before the `for` loop.

In the `for` loop, we could have separate branches for the label being empty or too long. This allows us to simply say things like `Requested domain ".example.com" is not a FQDN because it contains an empty label.` rather than including `""` and a less helpful error message.

I personally think this route is better, but if you decide to go this way, please also add tests for the new branches to https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/certbot/tests/util_test.py#L333 (looks like we're already missing some test coverage there). Sorry for the false start here, but I don't think we want this. I just created #3782 to explain what I think we should do with our Dockerfile, but to provide more context, I think we have three options:

1. Install from OS packages
2. Install from PyPI packages
3. Install from git

`git` has the significant benefit that we can easily automate builds on quay.io and DockerHub. When we create a new release tag, we can configure those sites to automatically build a new docker image. My plan is to have Docker tag for each release and have `latest` be for the most recent release. We could potentially include `master` tag for people who want it.

This isn't as feasible when using PyPI packages because in addition to being unable to provide a `master` tag, due to how `certbot-auto` works, we must create the GitHub tag before uploading to PyPI which creates a race condition. Furthermore, I think having a Dockerfile in our tree that doesn't use the files in that tree is a bit awkward and has no real benefit over simply using the local files.

The benefit of OS packages is we don't need any dependencies for compilation. I proposed in #3762 to try removing these dependencies after installing Certbot. The downside of using OS packages in our Dockerfile is we can't automate builds as they depend on when the packages are uploaded in the distro, we can't provide a `master` tag, and we still have the awkward behavior of having a Dockerfile in the tree that doesn't use the build context.

I hope this makes sense. If you have any questions, comments, concerns, etc. please follow up here or in #3762. I'm closing this issue, but if my current plan falls through for some reason, we can revisit this PR.

If you run `certbot renew` or `certbot certonly --non-interactive`, your certificate will not be renewed until it approaches expiration (30 days prior to expiration by default). While I'm glad the workaround there was successful, it's at least partially the cause of the error you're seeing now. `pip` is looking for `argparse` at that location because you told `pip` that URL was another package repository. I would expect `pip` to fall back to checking another location as well (unless you put `index-url` instead of `extra-index-url`), but `pip` should stop looking for `argparse` there if you remove that line from your `pip` configuration which should no longer be needed anyway based on my reading of bennuttall/piwheels#20.

I think it's at least worth testing without the prior workaround which should either resolve the problem or give us more information.

Not true; we may not have seen all parameters yet :-)

The battery indicator is based off of the SYS_STATUS message, and gets the information in the same way that the battery module does. If the battery remain reaches zero, then the bar is blacked out, indicating either that the battery % is zero or that there has been no battery information (through SYS_STATUS) being passed through since the startup of MAVProxy. I would assume that in this case this is due to the latter, as testing with Rover (and for me Plane) in SITL, the Battery percentage and current draw do not appear in the console module, while they do when running ArduCopter in SITL, matching the cases where the information is and isn't present in the horizon module.

The altimeter gauge is a good idea, I'll give it a shot at adding it at a later date. Shouldn't be too hard, would be similar to the pitch markers without the rotation and more numbers, but I might have to rearrange the layout a little bit to fit it.

Ah, yes the `cpu_limit` option [is a float](https://github.com/jupyterhub/jupyterhub/blob/3b07bd286b301b96ebb7b177bdae4ac6d1add42a/jupyterhub/spawner.py#L415). Sorry, I guess there's not a workaround currently then. I can submit a PR later today.

(by stephen-tiedemann)
This is not a Python version issue but related to libus/pyusb. The root cause seems to be that libusb fails to claim the interface for the USB device and returns some error code that the _check() method in pyusb wants to raise up the stack. Then conversion of the error code to an error string fails because the libusb dynamic library doesn't have the libusb_strerror function.

Without knowing what pyUSB wants to say about the problem with claim_interface, it's speculative to say what might have caused the issue first place. However, a common cause would be that some another process has already claimed it or there are insufficient access permissions.

CSV is not platform dependant and the produced CSV is valid (it's the output of Python CSV module left untouched):
- [RFC 4180](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc4180) tells that line returns should be `CRLF`
- cells content is left untouched but is enclosed in double-quotes if it contains `CR`, `LF` or `CRLF`

To validate the behavior, you can open the CSV in some dedicated tool, Libre Office, Python CSV module, Microsoft Excel **CSV import** (not the open function which only open Excel exported CSV right) and it should be properly read.
As example, try to read the produced CSV from http://www.convertcsv.com/csv-viewer-editor.htm (using the upload feature, not the URL which seems broken)

The simplest solution, if you fear errors in your dispatching code, is not to
use isinstance checks. Just check if the type matches. The above dispatching
code would become:

```
   if type(obj) == StopStateChange:
       ...
   elif type(obj) == StopWithCareStateChange:
       ...
```

If you have a deep class tree, functools.singledispatch would also be an option,
which can do the dispatching for you.

So, let's assume there are other reasons to forbid inheriting from those classes.

This concept is known from java as 'final class', i.e. a class can be marked
final, which means you cannot subclass it. The PR you contributed is different 
in that it seems to allow one level of subclassing and not more [2].

If you really need that, I propose to use a solution that works like java final
classes. See [1] for an implementation.

Though some would consider such a solution as unpythonic. 


[1] https://stackoverflow.com/questions/3948964/in-python-how-can-i-prohibit-class-inheritance
[2] The 'final declaration' for a class is hidden in it's superclass in your implementation. If you
   really need final classes, please mark those classes instead of marking the
   superclass as 'this class can be subclassed, but only one level deep'.

> The biggest problem I see with that approach is that we require every user to have the solc solidity compiler installed (with the right version).

I don't see a problem with this if the user is creating a virtual env and doing a source installation.

> We also have quite some code to check for a valid installed solc, compilation of contracts and in the packaged raiden versions we precompile the contract code anyways

I also don't think this is bad, people will try to use the `solc-js` and it won't work, it's better to have the checks in the code than letting the compiler fail.

Nevertheless, this would be my preferred approach:

- Every smart contract deployed should be available on the filesystem tree (not overwritten and burried in the commit history).
- The version deployed should be compiled and available along side the source code, with the resulting binary and the abi.
- The addresses of the deployed contracts should be available along side the source code and compiled artifacts.

Imagemagick as a `compare` tool that allegedly can help us with 1.
Something like:

```bash
$ compare -metric AE -fuzz 1% orig.png optimized.png diff.png
```

Obviously, it will not test all browsers/renders nor animations, but it
should catch visible artefacts in simple images (over the
"fuzz"-threshold - see [imagemagick-cmdline])


> 2. output size is smaller than input and previous version of Scour output
> 

Ok.  The scour tool has a bunch of options that control optimized size
at the price of visual artefacts.  Which options should we use for this
test?  Perhaps something like the following as the default?

```
  --enable-id-stripping
  --enable-comment-stripping
  --shorten-ids
  --indent=none
  --no-line-breaks
  --strip-xml-prolog
  --remove-descriptive-elements
  --set-precision=8 [--set-c-precision=8]
  --disable-embed-rasters
  --renderer-workaround
```


> [...]> do we expect Scour to retain pixel exact identity between the
rendering of the original SVG and the optimized SVG?
> 

Depends on the options used (e.g. the --set-precision is exactly
changing pixel identities).  However, with `compare` we can define an
"acceptable level of error" via the `-fuzz` parameter and then choose
the options so conservative that we stay inside that level of error.

` we cant have parameters with the same name in the same dataset/run (I think Its not a big deal, but different than what's now).`

Our measurements often use the same parameter multiple times in a single measurement. For example, one of our more complex parameters does a retuning sequence. Highlevel comment on both this and #622: this functionality is great, and obviously an important step towards #4. One question we need to answer along the way is how we're going to expose it – eventually it'll be part of some full-fledged subprocess spawning API, but should we expose the individual pieces, and if so, how? For `WaitForSingleObject`, this was pretty easy: it's an OS-specific core primitive, and the proper API was obvious, so, make it public in `trio.hazmat`, done. For these, it's a little more complicated: these particular implementations are specific to Unix/Linux, but the concepts are more general; and, I think we'll probably want to fine-tune the API as we go. (For example, with pipes we need to create a pipe where one end is wrapped in trio and non-blocking, and the other is in blocking mode to be passed to the child; and with `waitpid`, I suspect we might adjust how we track the `WaitpidResult` once we have `Popen` objects that could hold them – like maybe we'll kick off the `waitpid` call immediately when the process is created, and stash the `WaitpidResult` in the `Popen` object?)

So, my suggestion for right now is:

* Go ahead and add the implementations, maybe even in a subpackage like `trio/_subprocess/...`
* Go ahead and write the tests
* Don't export them publically
* Don't write newsfragments (because there's nothing for users yet)

That way we can incrementally write the different pieces, review them, test them, etc., and eventually we'll figure out how to hook them together into a coherent subprocess API.

PyCharm highlights the strings in `__all__` as unresolved because these names are defined via updating `globals()` a few lines below and PyCharm fails to evaluate it statically.

Mentioning names like `run` in `__all__` doesn't help PyCharm to see the name because `__all__` is redefined later via `+= ... .__all__` and we don't evaluate the imported `__all__` definitions. Instead we just ignore `__all__` and assume that all the names defined in the module are exported. But since `run` is not defined in this module from a static analyzer point of view (due to the use of the dynamic `globals()` update), we highlight `trio.run` as unresolved.

By the way, this is not just the limitation of PyCharm. Mypy also doesn't see `run` there:

```
mypy a.py --check-untyped-defs
a.py:3: error: Module has no attribute "run"
```

The easiest way to provide static information about your dynamic imports is to actually import all the stuff from `.core` inside a false check, including `hazmat` you don't want to import:

```python
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING

if TYPE_CHECKING:
    from ._core import *
```
or even:

```python
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING

if False:
    from ._core import *
```

although some analyzers would actually evaluate `False` to false and ignore this import.

@RaphaelMeudec @Sat0ri @mattdornfeld I've implemented that in my project, just take a look at:

https://github.com/guillem74/DRLDBackEnd/blob/master/callbacks.py#L179

Basically what I am doing is calling TrainEpisodeLogger callback when training. I modified the callback in order to compare after each episode if the reward is the highest at the moment, and if it is, I save the checkpoint weights. So basically, I have edited the on_episode_end() function by adding this:

        '''
        Code for saving up weights if the episode reward is higher than the last one
        '''
        
        if np.sum(self.rewards[episode])>self.lastreward:
            
            previousWeights = 'checkpoint_reward_{}.h5f'.format(self.lastreward)
            if os.path.exists(previousWeights): os.remove(previousWeights)
            self.lastreward = np.sum(self.rewards[episode])
            print("The reward is higher than the best one, saving checkpoint weights")
            newWeights = 'checkpoint_reward_{}.h5f'.format(np.sum(self.rewards[episode]))
            self.model.save_weights(newWeights, overwrite=True)
            
        else:
            print("The reward is lower than the best one, checkpoint weights not updated")
            
I also initialize self.lastreward = -200 in order to not save weights before -200 of episode reward.
